QA README 8,913 chars · 📘 QA README tab

CISSP Master HTML — QA & Exam-Authoring Deliverables Index

Engagement: autonomous Functional-Testing + Exam-Pattern-Analysis agent run, 10 h shift. Target under test: CISSP_Master.html (single-file portable CISSP study application). Owner: Raghu Prasad Last updated: 2026-05-13

This index ties off the two parallel tracks of the engagement:

  • Track A — Functional & Endurance Testing: a test plan, a 20-item defect log, and the legacy functional test report that preceded the v2 engine work.
  • Track B — Exam-Pattern Analysis & Original Question Authoring: per-domain pattern briefs and 80 paraphrased CISSP-style questions across all 8 domains.

All artefacts are in output/. Read them in the order below for the cleanest onboarding.

1. Recommended reading order

#ArtefactWhat it gives youTime
1🧪 QA Test Plan tabThe 10-section test matrix that drove Track A. Start here to understand the scope, severity scale, and triage workflow.10 min
2🐞 Defect Log tab20 defects (DEF-001 … DEF-020) in the mandatory 8-field format, plus 5 coverage gaps and a triage summary.25 min
3🎯 Track B Patterns tab8 per-domain pattern briefs + 80 original MCQs with rationales, distractor notes, and tags. Use for drill authoring.60 min
4📐 Platform Spec tabThe product specification this engine implements (interactive HTML version). Reference when triaging spec-vs-engine mismatches.30 min
5Functional Test Report (legacy, content merged here)Earlier functional test report (pre-v2 engine). Historical context for regression-tracking.10 min

2. Track A — Functional & Endurance Testing

2.1 🧪 QA Test Plan tab (12 KB, 167 lines)

Ten sections covering:

  1. Pre-flight (file size, runtime presence, embedded JSON sanity)
  2. Smoke & navigation (14 tabs, 8 domain panes, hash routing, theme/font, Ctrl+K palette)
  3. Core user journeys (mnemonics, cheats, scenarios, decoder, flashcards, settings)
  4. CAT engine validation — the highest-priority block (17 tests covering blueprint quotas, no-repeat, recent-30 exclusion, difficulty clamp, top-5 random pool, timers, history cap)
  5. Adaptive scoring & persistence (SM-2 transitions, EF floor, exportCSV, localStorage sizing)
  6. Endurance (3 back-to-back CAT-100s, 200 theme/font cycles, palette spam, idle behaviour)
  7. Negative & edge cases (refresh mid-session, regex meta-chars, >5-option items, picked-out-of-bounds, corrupted JSON, voice phonemes)
  8. Accessibility & UX (colour contrast, focus indicators, ARIA, reduced-motion, alert() abuse)
  9. Cross-cutting risk heuristics
  10. 90-minute schedule + mandatory defect-log format

Environment caveat baked into the plan: no live browser in the sandbox — tests run as static-code traces + dataset assertions. Tests requiring DOM/render verification are flagged [BROWSER-DEP] and logged as coverage gaps, not defects.

2.2 🐞 Defect Log tab (22 KB, 348 lines)

20 defects + 5 coverage gaps + triage summary. Each entry follows the mandatory eight-field schema (Title, Severity, Reproduction steps, Expected vs Actual, Impacted component, Frequency, Suspected root cause, Suggested improvement).

Triage

SeverityCountIDs
Blocker0—
Critical1DEF-003 (CAT-100 timer state not persisted across refresh)
Major6DEF-001, DEF-002, DEF-004, DEF-005, DEF-007, DEF-013
Minor13DEF-006, DEF-008–DEF-012, DEF-014–DEF-020
Coverage gap ([BROWSER-DEP])5G-01 … G-05

Top fixes by user impact

  1. DEF-003 — timer state lost on refresh (Critical) — persist STATE.session.timerEnd and rebind on init.
  2. DEF-001 — blueprint drift always dumped on D1 (Major) — adopt the largest-remainder method to rotate drift across domains.
  3. DEF-002 — recent-30 exclusion leaks across sessions (Major) — replace history-tail filter with per-item lastSeen timestamps and a configurable exposure window.
  4. DEF-013 — pickQuestion returns null silently when pool exhausted (Major) — add a relaxation ladder (widen difficulty → ignore recency → substitute nearest-weight domain).
  5. DEF-007 — keyboard shortcuts A–E only (Major) — extend to A–H / 1–8 so drag-drop and hotspot items are reachable.

2.3 Functional Test Report (legacy, content merged here) (10 KB, historical)

The pre-v2 functional test report retained for regression context. The current test plan supersedes it; entries here should be cross-referenced against the v2 engine paths (buildBlueprintPlan, pickQuestion, submitAnswer) when reproducing older findings.

3. Track B — Exam-Pattern Analysis & Original Question Bank

3.1 🎯 Track B Patterns tab (68 KB, 1,303 lines)

Structure

  • Per-domain pattern briefs (D1–D8): 5–6 archetypes each, plus wording cues (BEST / FIRST / MOST / Manager / Calculation / Definition) and common traps.
  • 80 original MCQs — 10 per domain — paraphrased to avoid copyright issues. Every item has: stem, 4 options, correct letter, rationale, distractor notes, and tags (domain, difficulty 2–4, pattern_type, bloom).
  • Cross-domain coverage table — pattern-type mix and Bloom span per domain.
  • Authoring recommendations for the next iteration of the master HTML question bank.

Coverage snapshot

DomainCBK weightItemsPattern types coveredBloom span
D1 — Risk & Governance16 %10Calculation, BEST, FIRST, MOST, Manager, Legal, Ethics, DefinitionRemember → Evaluate
D2 — Asset Security10 %10Definition, Manager, BEST, Legal, FIRST, MOSTRemember → Evaluate
D3 — Architecture & Engineering13 %10Definition, BEST, MOST, FIRSTRemember → Analyze
D4 — Network Security13 %10Definition, BEST, FIRST, MOSTRemember → Evaluate
D5 — IAM13 %10Definition, BEST, FIRST, MOST, ManagerRemember → Analyze
D6 — Assessment & Testing12 %10Definition, BEST, FIRST, MOST, ManagerRemember → Evaluate
D7 — Security Operations13 %10Definition, FIRST, BEST, MOST, ManagerRemember → Analyze
D8 — Software Dev Security10 %10BEST, FIRST, Definition, MOST, ManagerRemember → Evaluate
Total100 %80

Recommendations carried over to the next sweep

  1. Backfill 5–8 D1 quantitative items (ALE / ROSI / EF / ARO arithmetic).
  2. Add MOST / FIRST framing variants to existing BEST-framed items.
  3. Author drag-drop / hotspot residual to exercise DEF-007 (A–H keyboard).
  4. Tag every existing question-bank item with pattern_type for targeted drills.
  5. Build a 25-item smoke-mock drawn equally from the eight domain-10 sets — a 90-min, blueprint-conformant warm-up.

4. Supporting artefacts (reference only)

FilePurpose
CISSP_Master.html (2.3 MB)The application under test. Map of key code paths is documented in the defect log component references (lines 10299–11343 cover the CAT engine + v2 upgrades).
📐 Platform Spec tab (65 KB)Product specification expressed as a navigable HTML doc. Cross-reference whenever a defect involves "is this engine behaviour correct vs the spec?"
API spec (legacy artifact) (31 KB)OpenAPI surface — useful when authoring the future server-side companion.
DB schema (legacy artifact) (16 KB)Postgres schema for cohorts, attempts, items. Mirrors the localStorage shapes used today.
Sample exam template (legacy artifact) (2.7 KB)125-item template — domain weights, item-type mix, item-exposure window, scoring rule, readiness bands. Drives the CAT engine.
Sample questions JSON (legacy artifact) (6.7 KB)JSON form of the seed items.
Sample questions CSV (legacy artifact) (3.6 KB)CSV schema for bulk import (matches what Track B's 80 items can be exported into).
Sample users import CSV (legacy artifact) (1 KB)Sample cohort import — useful for future multi-user packaging.

5. Engagement state — what's done, what's open

Done

  • Test plan written and executed in static-trace mode (60–90 min sweep).
  • 20 defects logged with full reproduction context.
  • 80 original CISSP-style MCQs authored across all 8 domains with per-domain pattern briefs.
  • Triage and prioritisation completed; top-five user-impact fixes called out.

Open / next sweep

  • Live-browser verification of the 5 coverage gaps (G-01 … G-05) — colour contrast (axe-core), screen-reader announcement order (NVDA / VoiceOver), print stylesheet, touch-target sizes, IndexedDB fallback.
  • Defect-fix verification — once any of DEF-001 / DEF-002 / DEF-003 / DEF-013 land, re-run the CAT engine block.
  • Track B extensions — the five recommendations in §3.1 above.
  • Endurance re-run on freshly mutated state — post-CAT history, post-flag-set, post-theme-cycled, mid-due-queue.

End of deliverables index.

QA Test Plan 11,924 chars · 🧪 QA Test Plan tab

CISSP Master HTML — Functional & Endurance Test Plan (Track A)

Target artifact: /mnt/workspace/output/CISSP_Master.html (11,343 lines / 2.29 MB) Plan author: QA agent (autonomous) Plan date: 2026-05-13 Plan window: 60–90 minutes (initial pass) — full Track A logs extend across the 10-hour shift Source of truth: the uploaded HTML and its embedded JSON only

0. Pre-flight

StepActionPass criterion
0.1Glob /mnt/workspace/output/CISSP_Master.htmlFile exists, size > 2 MB
0.2wc -l CISSP_Master.html≥ 11,000 lines (CAT engine + v2 upgrades present)
0.3Grep "CISSP_RUNTIME"One definition at ~line 10750
0.4Grep "CISSP_UPGRADES_V2"One definition at ~line 11320
0.5Grep "buildBlueprintPlan"Definition + one caller in startSession
0.6Grep "question_bank"Embedded JSON, ≥ 700 items combined

Environment caveat: no live browser in the sandbox. All tests are executed as static-code traces + dataset assertions against the embedded JSON. Any test that genuinely requires DOM/render verification is flagged [BROWSER-DEP] and logged as a coverage gap rather than a defect — unless a clear logic flaw is visible in source.

1. Smoke & Navigation — 10 min

IDTestMethodExpected
S-01Top tab bar renders 14 buttonsGrep nav buttons in shell HTMLAll 14 ids present, no duplicates
S-02Domain panes 1–8 load from DOMAIN_DATATrace activate() against DOMAIN_DATA keysEach data-d{n} resolves to an embedded HTML blob
S-03Hash deep-links #1 … #8, #cat, #mn, #cs, #sc, #mdTrace router functionEach hash maps to a unique pane
S-04Theme toggle cycles dark → light → sepiaTrace cycleThemeThree classes applied in rotation, persisted to cissp_theme_v2
S-05Font step +/- (-3 … +5)Trace applyFontStepBounds enforced, persisted to cissp_font_step_v2
S-06Ctrl+K palette opensTrace init() keydownModal element shown, input focused
S-07Arrow / Enter / Escape inside paletteTrace handlersUp/Down moves selection, Enter activates, Escape closes
S-08Palette empty stateTrace filterPalette('')First 50 entries returned (truncated)
S-09Palette query resultsTrace filterPalette('crypto')Top 100 matches; ranking is index order, not relevance

IDTestMethodExpected
C-01Mnemonics pane rendersTrace renderMnemonics against CISSP_DATA.mnemonics8 cards, one per domain
C-02Cheats pane rendersTrace renderCheatsOne cheat sheet per domain
C-03Scenarios pane rendersTrace renderScenariosAll scenarios listed
C-04Manager's Decoder rendersTrace renderDecoderDecoder rows present
C-05Flashcard deck loadsGrep CISSP_DATA.flashcards≥ 200 cards
C-06Settings → Reset all clears localStorageTrace renderSettingslocalStorage.clear() invoked

3. CAT Engine Validation — 20 min (highest priority)

IDTestMethodExpected
CAT-01buildBlueprintPlan(100) quotasCompute Math.round(0.16100) … Math.round(0.10100)16,10,13,13,13,12,13,10 = 100 (rounded, no drift)
CAT-02buildBlueprintPlan(125) quotasCompute Math.round per blueprint20,12,16,16,16,15,16,12 = 123 → drift +2 dumped onto D1 → D1=22
CAT-03buildBlueprintPlan(150) quotasCompute Math.round24,15,20,20,20,18,20,15 = 152 → drift -2 → D1=22
CAT-04No-repeat within sessionTrace SESSION.served.has(q.id)Same q.id never returned twice
CAT-05Recent-30 exclusionTrace STATE.history.slice(-30) filterCross-session bleed: question shown in session N-1 cannot appear in session N
CAT-06Difficulty adaptation +/-1 clamp 1..5Trace submitAnswer theta_after computationcurrentDifficulty never <1 or >5
CAT-07Top-5 random poolTrace pickQuestion sort/sliceScore = -Math.abs(q.diff - td) + Math.random()*0.7; pick from top 5
CAT-08Plan exhausts before quotaTrace pickQuestion returning nullloadNextQuestion should call endSession; verify no infinite loop
CAT-09A–E keyboard shortcutsGrep init() keydown handlerKeys A,B,C,D,E and 1,2,3,4,5 mapped; F+ unhandled
CAT-10Enter submitsGrep Enter handlerTriggers click on focused .cat-opt
CAT-11Rationale visibility on submitTrace submitAnswer DOM mutationRationale block injected after click
CAT-12CAT100 timer initTrace timerEnd = Date.now() + 66000001h50m countdown
CAT-13CAT100 10-min warningTrace tickTimer rem <= 600.warning class added
CAT-14CAT100 auto-submit at 0Trace tickTimer rem === 0alert() + #cat-end-btn click
CAT-15History capped at 500Trace submitAnswer history.length > 500Oldest pushed off silently
CAT-16Practice mode never auto-endsTrace mode='practice' branchtarget not set → loadNextQuestion does not call endSession
CAT-17Weak-mode cold startTrace computeWeakDomain when all stats <3Returns null → CAT engine behaviour?

4. Adaptive / Scoring / Persistence — 10 min

IDTestMethodExpected
AD-01SM-2 wrong answerTrace sm2Update(qid,false)q=2, repetitions reset, interval=1, due = now + 1d
AD-02SM-2 first correctTrace sm2Update(qid,true) x1reps=1, interval=1
AD-03SM-2 second correctTrace x2reps=2, interval=6
AD-04SM-2 third correctTrace x3interval=round(prev*ef); ef floor 1.3
AD-05SM-2 EF floorTrace q=2 repeatedlyef never drops below 1.3
AD-06countDue()Trace iteration over cissp_sm2_v2Counts only entries with due ≤ now
AD-07Flag toggleTrace toggleFlagSets or deletes cissp_flags_v2[qid]
AD-08exportCSV escapingTrace exportCSVCommas/quotes/newlines escaped per RFC 4180
AD-09localStorage size growthEstimate cissp_cat_v2 size after 200 attempts< 200 KB (well under 5 MB quota)

5. Endurance — 20 min

IDScenarioMethodPass criterion
E-01Three back-to-back CAT-100 sessionsTrace session lifecycle, history growthHistory grows to ≥300 entries, no plan corruption
E-02200 rapid theme/font cyclesTrace cycleTheme / applyFontStepNo detached DOM listeners (static check: each binding is one-shot or idempotent)
E-03Palette open/close spam 100xTrace open/close handlersbuildPaletteIndex only runs once (cached forever — see DEFECT)
E-04setInterval(refreshProgress, 60000) running for 10hStatic checkNever cleared; 600 invocations over a 10h tab → battery cost noted
E-05Long idle (30 min) mid-CAT sessionTrace timer; no idle handlerTimer continues; no warning surfaced
E-06Rapid domain switching during CATTrace targetDomain setterpickQuestion honours latest target; no race
E-07MutationObserver firing rateTrace #cat-stem observerFires on every text change; no debounce (perf concern noted)

6. Negative & Edge Cases — 15 min

IDTestMethodExpected (per spec / sensible behaviour)
N-01Refresh mid-CAT-100 sessionTrace localStorage restoretimerEnd is in-memory only → timer NOT restored (defect)
N-02Palette query with regex meta-charsTrace filterPalette toLowerCase + includesSafe (uses .includes, not RegExp)
N-03Palette query 1000 charsTraceNo throw, but always returns 0 results
N-04Question with >5 options (item types in template: drag_drop, hotspot)Grep question bank for >5 optionsA–E shortcut limit excludes options F+ from keyboard control
N-05submitAnswer with picked >= options.lengthTrace input validationNo bounds check (defect)
N-06History truncation visibilityTrace history.length > 500 sliceSilent — no UI surface (defect)
N-07SM-2 with corrupted JSON in localStorageTrace loadSM2 try/catchFalls back to {}
N-08Voice input: phonemes A/ALPHA/EH onlyTrace startVoiceInput mappingF–H phonemes unrecognised
N-09TTS unavailable on host browserTrace 'speechSynthesis' in window guardGraceful degradation? (verify)
N-10Domain bucket smaller than allocationConstruct synthetic dataset; trace buildBlueprintPlanSilent truncation (defect)
N-11All-D1 dataset, targetDomain=3Trace pickQuestionReturns null → loadNextQuestion ends session
N-12Reset settings during active CAT sessionTrace reset flowActive session state cleared mid-flight (verify safeguard)

7. Accessibility & UX — 10 min

IDTestMethodExpected
A-01Colour contrast in dark/light/sepiaGrep CSS hex codes for body/text/linksAll foreground/background pairs ≥ 4.5:1 (WCAG AA)
A-02Focus indicators on .cat-optGrep :focus rulesVisible outline ≥ 3:1
A-03ARIA on CAT option buttonsGrep aria- attrsrole="button" or native <button>; aria-pressed after click
A-04Keyboard-only navTrace tab order in DOM orderAll controls reachable, no traps
A-05showHint uses alert()Grep usageUX defect: alert blocks main thread, breaks screen readers (defect)
A-06Reduced-motion preferenceGrep prefers-reduced-motionVerify motion-heavy elements honour the media query
A-07Screen-reader labels on iconsGrep aria-label on icon-only buttonsPresent
A-08Skip-to-content linkGrep <a href="#main">Optional but recommended

8. Cross-cutting Risk Heuristics — applies throughout

  • Silent data loss — any splice / slice(-N) / length > X truncation without UI surface = Major.
  • Cross-session bleed — anything that reads STATE.history for filtering future sessions = Major (regression on item exposure).
  • Race windows — any setTimeout(..., N) inside event handlers with N < 500ms = Minor (double-fire risk).
  • Permanent caches — any if (!cache) cache = build() with no invalidation hook = Minor.
  • Modal alert() usage — always Minor for UX, Major if blocks recovery flow.
  • Hash-only routing without popstate handler = Minor (back button breaks pane sync).
  • localStorage without quota probe = Minor (5 MB cap risk on extended use).

9. Schedule (60–90 min initial sweep)

BlockMinutesFocus
0:00–0:1010Pre-flight + Smoke & Navigation (§0, §1)
0:10–0:3020CAT Engine Validation (§3) — highest payoff
0:30–0:4010Adaptive / Scoring / Persistence (§4)
0:40–0:5515Negative & Edge Cases (§6)
0:55–1:1520Endurance (§5)
1:15–1:2510Accessibility & UX (§7)
1:25–1:305Triage, draft defect log entries, mark Track B switch-over point

After this initial sweep, Track A re-runs cycle through the same matrix on freshly mutated state (post-CAT history, post-flag-set, post-theme-cycled, mid-due-queue) to surface stateful defects. When a static-only check reaches a [BROWSER-DEP] ceiling, switch to Track B (exam-pattern analysis) and resume Track A when a new code path is ready to inspect.

10. Defect log format (mandatory)

Every defect entry MUST include:

  1. Title — short, action-oriented
  2. Severity — Blocker / Critical / Major / Minor
  3. Reproduction steps — numbered, code-level if applicable
  4. Expected vs Actual — both, side by side
  5. Impacted component — function name, line number(s), pane
  6. Frequency — Always / Often / Sometimes / Rare
  7. Suspected root cause — one-line hypothesis
  8. Suggested improvement — minimal patch idea

Defect log: 🐞 Defect Log tab

Defect Log 21,905 chars · 🐞 Defect Log tab

CISSP Master HTML — Defect Log (Track A)

Target: output/CISSP_Master.html (11,343 lines) Method: static code trace + dataset assertions (no live browser in sandbox) Severity scale: Blocker > Critical > Major > Minor Frequency scale: Always > Often > Sometimes > Rare

Defect format — mandatory fields: Title · Severity · Repro steps · Expected vs Actual · Impacted component · Frequency · Suspected root cause · Suggested improvement

DEF-001 — buildBlueprintPlan drift always dumped onto Domain 1

  • Severity: Major
  • Reproduction steps: 1. Open browser console (or static-trace). 2. Call window.CISSP_RUNTIME.buildBlueprintPlan(125). 3. Inspect plan length and per-domain counts.
  • Expected: A 125-item CAT plan should distribute rounding drift proportionally — or at minimum rotate which domain absorbs it — so that no single domain is consistently over- or under-represented across many sessions.
  • Actual: Math.round(0.16125)=20, 0.10125=13, 0.13125=16, 0.13125=16, 0.13125=16, 0.12125=15, 0.13125=16, 0.10125=13 → sum = 125; but for 100-item runs (16+10+13+13+13+12+13+10=100, OK) and 150-item runs (24+15+20+20+20+18+20+15=152, drift = −2) the entire drift is added to / subtracted from Domain 1 only. Code path: plan = plan.concat(Array(diff).fill(1)) if positive, or plan.pop() while D1 remains. Over many sessions, candidates see Domain 1 systematically over- or under-weighted vs the published blueprint.
  • Impacted component: buildBlueprintPlan() ~lines 10499–10527.
  • Frequency: Always (whenever total % blueprint rounding produces drift ≠ 0).
  • Suspected root cause: Single-bucket overflow handling — drift is not rotated across domains.
  • Suggested improvement: Round-robin the drift across domains in descending order of (weight - rounded/total) residual (largest-remainder method), or rotate the absorbing domain by session count.

DEF-002 — pickQuestion recent-30 exclusion leaks across sessions

  • Severity: Major
  • Reproduction steps: 1. Complete a CAT-100 session. 2. Immediately start a fresh CAT-100 session. 3. Observe that the first ~30 questions of the new session deliberately avoid items served in the previous session.
  • Expected: Item-exposure throttling should be a configurable window (default 30 days per the spec) backed by per-item timestamps, not the last 30 entries of STATE.history (which conflates "recently served in any session" with "exposure-controlled").
  • Actual: pickQuestion reads STATE.history.slice(-30) as the exclusion set, where STATE.history is the cross-session history capped at 500. This creates a deterministic ~30-question cooldown that bleeds across CAT sessions and silently shrinks the available pool for short, focused drills.
  • Impacted component: pickQuestion() ~lines 10529–10554.
  • Frequency: Always.
  • Suspected root cause: Conflation of session-scoped repeat protection (SESSION.served) with longer-term exposure throttling (which the spec puts at 30 days).
  • Suggested improvement: Maintain lastSeen[qid] = ts in cissp_exposure_v2; exclude items where now - lastSeen[qid] < EXPOSURE_WINDOW_MS (default 30 days). Keep SESSION.served as the in-session repeat guard.

DEF-003 — CAT-100 timer state not persisted across page refresh

  • Severity: Critical
  • Reproduction steps: 1. Start a CAT-100 session at t=0. 2. Answer ~10 questions over 5 minutes. 3. Refresh the page (Ctrl+R / F5). 4. Resume CAT pane.
  • Expected: Timer resumes from where it left off (i.e., 1h45m remaining), since STATE persists in localStorage and the session is mid-flight.
  • Actual: timerEnd = Date.now() + 6600000 is computed in-memory inside startSession-adjacent code and never written to localStorage. On refresh, tickTimer either fails to start (no timerEnd rebound) or restarts the full clock if the user re-clicks Start.
  • Impacted component: CAT-100 timer block ~lines 10909–10936; loadCatState/saveCatState ~lines 10850–10860.
  • Frequency: Always on refresh during active CAT-100.
  • Suspected root cause: timerEnd lives only on module scope, not in the persisted session state.
  • Suggested improvement: Persist STATE.session.timerEnd at session start; on init(), if a session is in-progress, rebind timerEnd from state and resume tickTimer. Also persist STATE.session.served array for the same reason.

DEF-004 — showHint uses blocking alert() dialog

  • Severity: Major
  • Reproduction steps: 1. During any CAT question, click the Hint button. 2. Observe a native alert() modal blocking the page.
  • Expected: Hint appears as an inline, dismissible tooltip / box near the question stem, non-blocking, accessible to screen readers.
  • Actual: alert(hint) blocks the main thread, halts TTS narration, can't be styled, and is announced abruptly by some assistive tech. On keyboard-only flows the focus jumps to the modal and back, disorienting navigation.
  • Impacted component: showHint() ~lines 10964–10980.
  • Frequency: Always when hint is invoked.
  • Suspected root cause: Quick implementation using native dialog rather than custom UI.
  • Suggested improvement: Render hint inside a <div role="status" aria-live="polite"> slotted under the question stem, with a close button. Preserve focus.

DEF-005 — submitAnswer does not validate picked against q.options.length

  • Severity: Major
  • Reproduction steps: 1. From console, invoke submitAnswer(currentQ, 7) on a 4-option question. 2. Observe state change.
  • Expected: Reject (or clamp) picked indices outside [0, q.options.length - 1], log a warning.
  • Actual: Function records picked=7 to history, computes is_correct against q.answer (which won't match), and proceeds. Downstream analytics (p-value, discrimination) become noisy on any item type that accepts more than the keyboard-bound A–E (e.g., 8-option drag/drop).
  • Impacted component: submitAnswer() ~lines 10612–10640.
  • Frequency: Sometimes (only reachable via direct DOM manipulation or future item types).
  • Suspected root cause: Missing input-validation guard.
  • Suggested improvement: if (picked < 0 || picked >= q.options.length) { console.warn(...); return; } at top of function.

DEF-006 — STATE.history silently truncated at 500

  • Severity: Minor
  • Reproduction steps: 1. Complete ≥ 5 CAT-100 sessions in a row. 2. Observe history length stuck at 500; oldest entries quietly dropped.
  • Expected: Either (a) larger cap with archive to IndexedDB / file export, or (b) user-visible notice "Older attempts archived — export CSV before they roll off."
  • Actual: if (history.length > 500) history.shift() (or equivalent) executes silently. Users running long study programs lose week-1 baseline attempts.
  • Impacted component: submitAnswer() ~line 10637 region.
  • Frequency: Sometimes (only on long users).
  • Suspected root cause: Arbitrary cap to bound localStorage growth.
  • Suggested improvement: Raise cap to ~2,000 (still well under 5 MB quota); surface a soft warning when ≥ 90 % of cap; expose exportCSV button prominently when nearing cap.

DEF-007 — Keyboard shortcuts A–E only; item types with 6–8 options unreachable

  • Severity: Major
  • Reproduction steps: 1. Inspect init() keydown handler (~line 10750). 2. Note keys A,B,C,D,E (+1,2,3,4,5) mapped; F–H absent. 3. Inspect Sample exam template (legacy artifact) — item_type_mix includes drag_drop and hotspot.
  • Expected: Keyboard shortcuts span the full option range of the largest item type the platform serves (template schema allows 2–8 options).
  • Actual: Keys F–H are silently dropped; users must mouse-click options 6–8. Voice-input phonetic mapping has the same ceiling.
  • Impacted component: init() keydown ~line 10750; startVoiceInput ~lines 11005–11027.
  • Frequency: Sometimes (only on items with >5 options).
  • Suspected root cause: Original engine targeted MCQ-only (4 or 5 options); was not extended when drag/drop and hotspot item types were added to the schema.
  • Suggested improvement: Map A–H and 1–8; for letters, generate the option list from q.options.length rather than hard-coding.

DEF-008 — Ctrl+K palette results silently truncated to 50 / 100

  • Severity: Minor
  • Reproduction steps: 1. Press Ctrl+K, leave query empty → 50 results. 2. Type a common term (e.g. "risk") → 100 results.
  • Expected: When more results exist than the slice limit, surface "Showing 100 of N — refine your query" footer.
  • Actual: filterPalette slices to 50/100 with no UI indication. Users searching broad terms believe nothing else matches.
  • Impacted component: filterPalette ~lines 11080–11110.
  • Frequency: Often.
  • Suspected root cause: Performance hardening without UX surface.
  • Suggested improvement: Return {items, total} from the filter; render a "Showing X of Y" footer when truncated.

DEF-009 — buildPaletteIndex cached for the entire page life — no invalidation

  • Severity: Minor
  • Reproduction steps: 1. Open Ctrl+K palette (builds + caches index). 2. (Hypothetical) flashcard / question state mutates. 3. Open palette again.
  • Expected: Index reflects current state; rebuilds if underlying data changes (or at minimum, has a documented refresh hook).
  • Actual: if (!PALETTE_INDEX) PALETTE_INDEX = buildPaletteIndex(); with no invalidation. Newly authored or imported content (if ever supported) would not surface in the palette until full page reload.
  • Impacted component: buildPaletteIndex / openPalette ~lines 11067–11192.
  • Frequency: Rare today (content is static), would become Often if content mutation lands.
  • Suspected root cause: Optimistic single-build cache.
  • Suggested improvement: Add __palette_dirty flag set by content mutation hooks; check at open time. Or rebuild on demand if any of the source arrays has changed length.

DEF-010 — refreshProgress runs every 60 s for the life of the tab (no clearInterval)

  • Severity: Minor
  • Reproduction steps: 1. Open page, leave tab open for 10 hours (intended endurance use). 2. Profile CPU / battery.
  • Expected: Pause / throttle when document is hidden (visibilitychange), or use requestIdleCallback.
  • Actual: setInterval(refreshProgress, 60000) runs unconditionally for the page life; on mobile or laptop on battery, this is wasted work when the tab is backgrounded.
  • Impacted component: init() ~line 11329.
  • Frequency: Always.
  • Suspected root cause: No visibility-aware scheduling.
  • Suggested improvement: document.addEventListener('visibilitychange', () => document.hidden ? clearInterval(t) : t = setInterval(refreshProgress, 60000)).

DEF-011 — Click-delegated sm2Update fires after 200 ms timeout — race on rapid double-click

  • Severity: Minor
  • Reproduction steps: 1. On any CAT question, double-click an option button quickly. 2. Observe cissp_sm2_v2 state for that qid.
  • Expected: Single SM-2 update per answered item.
  • Actual: Document-level click delegation calls setTimeout(() => sm2Update(qid, correct), 200). A rapid second click within 200 ms — common on touchpads or with sticky keys — would queue a second sm2Update for the same item; second invocation's r.repetitions += 1 advances the interval erroneously.
  • Impacted component: Document click delegate ~lines 11280–11300; sm2Update ~lines 10866–10881.
  • Frequency: Rare (requires fast double-click on the same option).
  • Suspected root cause: Asynchronous delay introduced to wait for DOM mutation to commit, without guarding against repeat events.
  • Suggested improvement: Track __sm2_in_flight[qid] = true for the timeout window; bail if already in flight. Or move sm2Update synchronous and call inside the option-click handler directly.

DEF-012 — MutationObserver on #cat-stem fires on every text mutation; no debounce

  • Severity: Minor
  • Reproduction steps: 1. Start CAT session. 2. With TTS active, the stem text might be styled / highlighted word-by-word. 3. Observe window.__cissp_current_q capture frequency.
  • Expected: Capture runs once per question render (on childList add), not on every character or style change.
  • Actual: Observer fires on broad mutations; __cissp_current_q is re-derived repeatedly. Wasted CPU; potentially serves stale q if mid-mutation.
  • Impacted component: init() MutationObserver block ~lines 11305–11320.
  • Frequency: Often during TTS playback or animation.
  • Suspected root cause: Observer configured with overly broad mutation types or no if (mutation.type === 'childList') guard.
  • Suggested improvement: Narrow observe config to { childList: true, subtree: false }; debounce capture with requestAnimationFrame.

DEF-013 — pickQuestion may return null silently if domain bucket exhausted

  • Severity: Major
  • Reproduction steps: 1. Construct a synthetic plan that asks for 22 D1 items. 2. Filter underlying bank to a pool with only 18 D1 items in the difficulty band. 3. Trace pickQuestion calls 19–22.
  • Expected: Engine surfaces a "pool exhausted" message and either widens difficulty band or substitutes from nearest domain.
  • Actual: pickQuestion returns null; loadNextQuestion (~line 10595) treats null as "done" and ends the session early. User receives a CAT-100 session of <100 items with no explanation.
  • Impacted component: pickQuestion() ~lines 10529–10554; loadNextQuestion() ~lines 10583–10595.
  • Frequency: Sometimes (depends on bank composition vs target plan).
  • Suspected root cause: No fallback strategy when domain bucket smaller than allocation.
  • Suggested improvement: Implement a relaxation ladder — widen difficulty band → ignore recent-30 exclusion → substitute with nearest-weight domain. Always emit a console warning and a per-session diagnostic.

DEF-014 — weak-mode cold start (all stats <3) — engine behaviour undocumented

  • Severity: Minor
  • Reproduction steps: 1. Fresh install (localStorage cleared). 2. Click weak mode.
  • Expected: Either (a) refuse with "Complete a quick drill first to identify your weak area" toast, or (b) fall back to lowest-weight domain by blueprint (D2/D8 at 10 %).
  • Actual: computeWeakDomain (~line 10556) returns null when no domain has ≥3 attempts; startSession('weak') would set targetDomain=null → behaviour resembles practice mode but is labelled "weak", confusing users.
  • Impacted component: computeWeakDomain ~lines 10556–10571; startSession ~lines 10573–10582.
  • Frequency: Always on first weak-mode click of a fresh install.
  • Suspected root cause: Cold-start path not designed.
  • Suggested improvement: When computeWeakDomain()===null, show banner "Not enough data yet — try Quick 20 first" and disable the weak button.

DEF-015 — Theme/font preferences not honoured for users who arrive via deep link

  • Severity: Minor
  • Reproduction steps: 1. Set theme to dark, font step to +2. 2. Copy URL with #cat and reload.
  • Expected: Stored prefs applied before pane mount.
  • Actual: applyTheme / applyFontStep are called in init(), which runs after initial DOM paint — there is a brief FOUC (flash of unstyled content) on slower devices. Static check.
  • Impacted component: init() ~line 11195.
  • Frequency: Often on slow connections / mobile.
  • Suspected root cause: Theme application deferred to DOMContentLoaded.
  • Suggested improvement: Inline a tiny pre-DOMContentLoaded script in <head> that reads cissp_theme_v2 and cissp_font_step_v2 from localStorage and sets the body class before paint.

DEF-016 — exportCSV does not include theta_after or timer_ms — analytics gap

  • Severity: Minor
  • Reproduction steps: 1. Complete a CAT session. 2. Trigger exportCSV. 3. Inspect file columns.
  • Expected: Per the spec §11.1 sample item analytics, the CSV should include enough to compute response-time distributions and theta trajectory.
  • Actual: Columns are ts,domain,question_id,difficulty,correct,answer. Missing: time_ms (captured in history but not exported), theta_after, mode, session_id.
  • Impacted component: exportCSV() ~lines 10939–10961.
  • Frequency: Always.
  • Suspected root cause: Initial export schema was minimal MVP.
  • Suggested improvement: Extend the header to ts,session_id,mode,domain,question_id,difficulty,correct,answer,time_ms,theta_after; re-use the same escape routine.

DEF-017 — Hash-based pane switching has no popstate handler

  • Severity: Minor
  • Reproduction steps: 1. Click through domains 1 → 2 → 3. 2. Press browser Back.
  • Expected: Pane returns to Domain 2 view.
  • Actual: URL hash updates but activate() is not re-invoked on popstate (only on click). Pane and URL drift out of sync.
  • Impacted component: activate() ~line 10336.
  • Frequency: Always on Back/Forward.
  • Suspected root cause: Missing window.addEventListener('popstate', () => activate(location.hash.slice(1) || '1')).
  • Suggested improvement: Add the listener; idempotent against current activate() implementation.

DEF-018 — Voice input falls silently when SpeechRecognition unavailable

  • Severity: Minor
  • Reproduction steps: 1. Open file in a browser without SpeechRecognition / webkitSpeechRecognition (e.g. Firefox). 2. Click the voice button.
  • Expected: Disable / hide voice button with tooltip "Voice input not supported in this browser."
  • Actual: startVoiceInput (~line 11005) likely no-ops or throws silently. The button remains clickable and users get no feedback.
  • Impacted component: startVoiceInput() and the button-binding code.
  • Frequency: Always on Firefox / Safari iOS (depending on version).
  • Suspected root cause: Feature-detection only at call time, not at button-render time.
  • Suggested improvement: On init(), feature-detect once; if absent, hide the voice button and skip the binding.

DEF-019 — CAT100 auto-submit relies on alert() then click — fragile under user dismissal

  • Severity: Minor
  • Reproduction steps: 1. Start CAT-100; wait for timer to expire. 2. Observe alert() fires, then endBtn.click(). 3. If alert() is dismissed before the JS engine reaches click() (rare timing), session may not finalize.
  • Expected: Submit deterministically without depending on user action.
  • Actual: Order is stopTimer(); alert(...); endBtn?.click(); — synchronous so usually OK, but if #cat-end-btn is absent (rare DOM mutation) the session is never ended cleanly; user sees alert but state shows in-progress.
  • Impacted component: tickTimer() ~lines 10930–10935.
  • Frequency: Rare.
  • Suspected root cause: Coupling to DOM button rather than directly calling endSession().
  • Suggested improvement: Call endSession() directly; alert after the state mutation.

DEF-020 — cissp_cat_v2 schema has no version field — future migration risk

  • Severity: Minor
  • Reproduction steps: 1. Inspect loadCatState default: {stats:{attempts:0,correct:0}}. 2. Note absence of schemaVersion.
  • Expected: Stored blob includes schemaVersion: N so a future code update can migrate older users without data loss.
  • Actual: No version field. Any future schema change must either accept dirty reads or wipe state.
  • Impacted component: loadCatState / saveCatState ~lines 10850–10860.
  • Frequency: Latent — fires when next breaking change ships.
  • Suspected root cause: v2 was the first migration; v3 anticipation not built in.
  • Suggested improvement: Add schemaVersion: 2 on every saveCatState; in loadCatState, run a migration switch on read.

Coverage gaps (NOT defects — flagged as [BROWSER-DEP])

  • G-01 — Live colour-contrast verification: static CSS scan covers known token pairs, but actual rendered contrast depends on font weights, anti-aliasing, and any future ad-hoc inline styles. Recommend a one-shot axe-core run when a browser is available.
  • G-02 — Screen-reader announcement order: order of role/aria-live and visual order must be compared with NVDA / VoiceOver in person.
  • G-03 — Native print stylesheet: spec calls for watermarked PDFs; the page contains no @media print rules — confirmed by Grep, but visual print preview wasn't done.
  • G-04 — Touch-target sizes on mobile: static check of .cat-opt padding suggests ~40 px; needs device measurement at common viewport widths (360, 390, 414).
  • G-05 — IndexedDB fallback: localStorage 5 MB cap may bite Domain-7-style heavy users; cannot live-test without browser.

Triage summary

SeverityCountIDs
Blocker0—
Critical1DEF-003
Major6DEF-001, DEF-002, DEF-004, DEF-005, DEF-007, DEF-013
Minor13DEF-006, DEF-008, DEF-009, DEF-010, DEF-011, DEF-012, DEF-014, DEF-015, DEF-016, DEF-017, DEF-018, DEF-019, DEF-020
Coverage gaps5G-01–G-05

Next sweep priorities (when fresh code paths or browser become available):

  1. Live verify DEF-003 (timer reload) — single-line fix, highest user-visible impact.
  2. Synthesise CAT-1000 history to confirm DEF-006 silent truncation behaviour.
  3. Construct adversarial dataset for DEF-001 fairness over 50 simulated sessions.
  4. Switch to Track B and begin Domain 1 exam-pattern analysis + question generation while CAT engine fixes are in flight.
Track B Patterns 67,475 chars · 🎯 Track B Patterns tab

CISSP Track B — Exam-Pattern Analysis & Original Question Bank

Source of truth: the uploaded CISSP_Master.html and its embedded JSON only — augmented by patterns visible in the embedded question_bank.json and the per-domain content blobs already authored in the master file. Scope: 8 domains × (pattern brief + 10 original questions). Total target ≈ 80 questions. Author: QA agent (autonomous) Date: 2026-05-13 Style guarantee: All scenarios are paraphrased/synthesised; no source text reproduced verbatim. Each question is tagged with domain, difficulty (1–5), pattern_type (BEST / FIRST / MOST / Manager / Calculation / Definition), and bloom. Answers cite the conceptual primitive being tested, not a verbatim source.

How to read the answer key:

  • Correct: B — letter is the right option.
  • Rationale — explains why the correct option wins.
  • Distractor notes — why each wrong option is wrong (the value of CISSP study).

Domain 1 — Security and Risk Management (16 %)

Pattern brief

Domain 1 is the governance-and-judgement domain. The exam tests whether you can think like a security leader — not whether you can recite definitions. Five archetypes recur:

  1. Risk arithmetic — SLE = AV × EF; ALE = SLE × ARO; ROSI = (ALE_before − ALE_after − cost) / cost. Always check the units. Common distractor: substituting EF for ARO or returning SLE when ALE is asked.
  2. Governance vs management — governance is direction-setting (policy, risk appetite, board oversight); management is execution (procedures, controls, day-to-day). When a question asks "who should approve …", the answer is usually senior management or the board, not the CISO.
  3. Due care vs due diligence — due diligence is the investigation / ongoing monitoring; due care is the action a prudent person would take. The classic wording cue: "before signing the contract" (diligence) vs "after the contract is signed" (care).
  4. Legal / regulatory priority — when laws or contracts conflict with internal policy, legal obligations win. Notification timelines (GDPR 72 h, HIPAA 60 days, PCI DSS as per merchant level) are favourite trick details.
  5. Ethics canons — the ISC2 canons are ordered: (1) protect society / common good / public trust / infrastructure; (2) act honourably, honestly, justly, responsibly, and legally; (3) provide diligent and competent service to principals; (4) advance and protect the profession. When the answer depends on canon priority, society first.

Wording cues: "BEST" → balance cost, risk, and feasibility. "FIRST" → the immediate next step in a process (usually risk-assessment-before-control). "MOST important" → executive-level judgement, often governance. "Manager would …" → policy / risk-acceptance / escalation answer, not a technical control.

Common traps:

  • Picking the most secure control over the most appropriate control. CISSP wants risk-proportionate, not maximalist.
  • Treating asset value as replacement cost only. AV must include reputational, regulatory, and operational impact.
  • Forgetting that risk transfer (insurance, outsourcing) does not transfer accountability.

Q-D1-01 (difficulty 2, Apply, Calculation)

A retail company values its customer-data store at $1,500,000. A ransomware threat is assessed with an exposure factor of 40 % and an annualised rate of occurrence of 0.25. What is the annualised loss expectancy?

  • A. $150,000
  • B. $375,000
  • C. $600,000
  • D. $1,500,000

Correct: A Rationale: SLE = AV × EF = 1,500,000 × 0.40 = 600,000. ALE = SLE × ARO = 600,000 × 0.25 = 150,000. Distractor notes: (B) substitutes EF for ARO; (C) is the SLE; (D) is the asset value. Tags: domain=1, difficulty=2, pattern=Calculation, bloom=Apply

Q-D1-02 (difficulty 3, Apply, BEST)

A mid-sized company is debating whether to spend $80,000 / year on a control that would reduce a current ALE of $220,000 to $60,000. Which statement BEST captures the decision the security manager should bring to the executive?

  • A. Implement the control because any reduction in ALE is a net positive.
  • B. Reject the control because $80,000 is a large recurring spend.
  • C. Recommend implementation: ROSI is roughly 1.0 ((220k − 60k − 80k) / 80k) — the control more than pays for itself.
  • D. Defer the decision until a quantitative risk assessment with a 95 % confidence interval is performed.

Correct: C Rationale: ROSI = (ALE_before − ALE_after − annual cost) / annual cost = (220,000 − 60,000 − 80,000) / 80,000 = 1.0 (100 % return). Manager should present the math and a recommendation. Distractor notes: (A) ignores cost; (B) ignores the saved loss; (D) over-engineers a clear-cut case. Tags: domain=1, difficulty=3, pattern=BEST,Calculation, bloom=Apply

Q-D1-03 (difficulty 3, Analyze, FIRST)

A new third-party vendor will process customer PII on behalf of your company. Before the contract is signed, which activity should be performed FIRST?

  • A. Sign a non-disclosure agreement and start onboarding.
  • B. Conduct due-diligence review of the vendor's security posture, certifications, and breach history.
  • C. Insert a right-to-audit clause and a breach-notification SLA into the master service agreement.
  • D. Request a SOC 2 Type II report and accept its findings without further review.

Correct: B Rationale: Due diligence is the investigation that precedes contractual commitment. (C) is correct content but happens after, or as the result of, diligence. (A) skips the assessment; (D) abdicates judgement. Tags: domain=1, difficulty=3, pattern=FIRST, bloom=Analyze

Q-D1-04 (difficulty 2, Understand, Definition)

Risk transfer through cyber-insurance shifts which of the following?

  • A. The accountability for safeguarding the data.
  • B. The legal obligation to notify regulators of a breach.
  • C. The financial impact of a covered loss, up to the policy limits and conditions.
  • D. All residual risk to the insurer, regardless of the cause.

Correct: C Rationale: Insurance redistributes financial impact; accountability, regulatory obligations, and uncovered residual risk remain with the data owner. Distractor notes: (A) and (B) are the classic "accountability is not transferable" trap. (D) ignores policy conditions and exclusions. Tags: domain=1, difficulty=2, pattern=Definition, bloom=Understand

Q-D1-05 (difficulty 4, Evaluate, MOST)

A CISSP-certified consultant discovers, while testing a client system, that the client is intentionally falsifying revenue figures in a way that materially misleads investors. Per the ISC2 Code of Ethics, what is the MOST appropriate action?

  • A. Continue the engagement; the financial fraud is outside the scope of the security test.
  • B. Report the matter to law enforcement immediately, bypassing the client entirely.
  • C. Raise the matter through the client's audit committee and, if not addressed, fulfil the canon to protect society — including reporting through appropriate channels.
  • D. Resign from the engagement quietly and take no further action.

Correct: C Rationale: Canon 1 (protect society / common good / public trust) outranks canon 3 (diligent service to principals). Escalation through governance first is the prudent path; persistent inaction triggers the higher canon. (A) ignores the canons; (B) skips legitimate channels; (D) chooses silence over the canon. Tags: domain=1, difficulty=4, pattern=MOST,Ethics, bloom=Evaluate

Q-D1-06 (difficulty 3, Apply, Manager)

You are the new CISO of a fintech that has never had a formal information-security policy. Which document should be produced FIRST?

  • A. A standard mandating AES-256 for all data-at-rest.
  • B. A high-level information-security policy approved by the board, stating intent, scope, and accountability.
  • C. A procedure for handling forgotten-password resets at the help desk.
  • D. A guideline recommending specific endpoint-protection vendors.

Correct: B Rationale: Hierarchy of policy → standard → procedure / guideline. The board-approved policy anchors everything downstream. (A) is a standard, (C) a procedure, (D) a guideline. Tags: domain=1, difficulty=3, pattern=FIRST,Manager, bloom=Apply

Q-D1-07 (difficulty 3, Apply, Legal)

Your organisation suffers a confirmed personal-data breach involving EU data subjects. Under GDPR, you must notify the supervisory authority within how many hours of becoming aware?

  • A. 24
  • B. 48
  • C. 72
  • D. 96

Correct: C Rationale: GDPR Article 33: notification to supervisory authority "not later than 72 hours after having become aware". 24 / 48 / 96 are common distractor durations from other regimes. Tags: domain=1, difficulty=3, pattern=Legal, bloom=Apply

Q-D1-08 (difficulty 4, Analyze, BEST)

A company's quantitative risk assessment shows that a particular threat has an ALE of $40,000. A proposed control costs $60,000 per year and reduces the ALE to $5,000. The CFO asks the CISO whether to implement it. The CISO's BEST response is:

  • A. "Implement the control — security is worth the investment regardless of cost."
  • B. "Do not implement the control as-is — annual cost exceeds annual loss reduction. We should evaluate cheaper alternatives, risk acceptance, or transfer."
  • C. "Defer the decision; we need a five-year discounted cash-flow analysis before deciding."
  • D. "Accept the risk silently — quantitative risk methods are unreliable."

Correct: B Rationale: Saved ALE = 40,000 − 5,000 = 35,000; cost = 60,000 → net −25,000 / year. Risk-proportionate response is to seek cheaper options, accept, or transfer. (A) is maximalist; (C) over-engineers a clear answer; (D) abandons the framework. Tags: domain=1, difficulty=4, pattern=BEST,Calculation, bloom=Analyze

Q-D1-09 (difficulty 2, Understand, Definition)

The principle of separation of duties is best illustrated by which of the following?

  • A. Forcing every administrator to change passwords every 30 days.
  • B. Requiring that the person who initiates a wire transfer is not the same person who approves it.
  • C. Rotating staff through all roles in the team every six months.
  • D. Encrypting databases at rest using a hardware security module.

Correct: B Rationale: Separation of duties splits a sensitive transaction across two or more individuals so that fraud requires collusion. (C) is rotation of duties (a different control); (A) is password hygiene; (D) is a technical confidentiality control. Tags: domain=1, difficulty=2, pattern=Definition, bloom=Understand

Q-D1-10 (difficulty 4, Evaluate, MOST)

After a major incident, the executive team asks the CISO who owns the residual risk of running a legacy unsupported operating system to support a critical revenue stream. The MOST accurate answer is:

  • A. The CISO, because all security risk belongs to the security function.
  • B. The IT operations manager, because they run the system.
  • C. The business owner of the revenue stream, because the business decision to keep the system in production creates the risk.
  • D. The vendor of the legacy operating system, because they ended support.

Correct: C Rationale: Risk ownership follows the business decision-maker who derives benefit and accepts the trade-off. The CISO advises; ops operates; the vendor's end-of-life status is an input, not an owner of residual risk. Tags: domain=1, difficulty=4, pattern=MOST,Manager, bloom=Evaluate

Domain 2 — Asset Security (10 %)

Pattern brief

Domain 2 is the data-lifecycle domain. Five archetypes:

  1. Classification & ownership — owner sets classification, custodian implements it, user abides. Re-classification on aggregation is a favourite trick.
  2. Data states — at rest / in transit / in use. Each state has a canonical protection (encryption / TLS / enclaves or DRM).
  3. Retention & disposal — minimum retention for regulation, maximum retention for privacy. Disposal methods follow media type (degauss for magnetic, crypto-erase for SED, physical destruction for SSD where assured).
  4. Data sovereignty — the data is subject to the law of the jurisdiction where it is stored and processed, not only where the company is headquartered.
  5. Roles — Data Owner (business), Data Custodian (IT), Data Steward (quality), Data Processor (handles on behalf), Data Subject (the person the data is about), DPO (advises on privacy).

Wording cues: "appropriate level of protection" → classification-driven control. "What should the data owner do?" → classification, accountability, approve access. "Securely dispose of …" → method depends on media + classification.

Q-D2-01 (difficulty 2, Understand, Definition)

Two non-sensitive datasets are merged into a single analytics warehouse. After the merge, the combined dataset would allow an attacker to identify individuals. The MOST appropriate response is:

  • A. Continue treating the dataset as non-sensitive because each source was non-sensitive.
  • B. Re-classify the merged dataset at the higher protection level dictated by the aggregation risk.
  • C. Delete the warehouse — aggregation is forbidden.
  • D. Encrypt one of the source datasets at rest before merging.

Correct: B Rationale: Aggregation changes the sensitivity profile and requires re-classification. (A) ignores the new risk; (C) is maximalist; (D) does not address the aggregation problem. Tags: domain=2, difficulty=2, pattern=Definition, bloom=Understand

Q-D2-02 (difficulty 2, Understand, Manager)

Who is accountable for classifying a dataset?

  • A. The Data Custodian (IT operations).
  • B. The Data Owner (business function that creates or holds the data).
  • C. The CISO.
  • D. The Data Steward.

Correct: B Rationale: Ownership = accountability for classification and access decisions. Custodian implements; CISO advises; Steward focuses on quality and meaning. Tags: domain=2, difficulty=2, pattern=Manager, bloom=Understand

Q-D2-03 (difficulty 3, Apply, BEST)

A laptop fleet uses self-encrypting drives (SEDs). When a laptop is retired, which sanitisation method is BEST?

  • A. Reformat the drive using the operating-system installer.
  • B. Issue a cryptographic erase (destroy the data-encrypting key) following the manufacturer's procedure.
  • C. Physically shred every drive regardless of cost or environmental impact.
  • D. Overwrite the drive seven times with random data.

Correct: B Rationale: Cryptographic erase on SED is fast, assured (the ciphertext becomes unrecoverable), and NIST-recognised. Physical destruction is acceptable but unnecessary; seven-pass overwrite does not address remapped sectors on SSD. Tags: domain=2, difficulty=3, pattern=BEST, bloom=Apply

Q-D2-04 (difficulty 3, Apply, Legal)

A European subsidiary of a US-headquartered company plans to store EU customer data in a US-based data centre. What is the MOST important consideration?

  • A. Local data-protection law applies based on where the data subjects reside and where the data is processed — adequate transfer mechanisms (e.g., SCCs, adequacy decisions) are required.
  • B. Only US law applies because the parent company is US-headquartered.
  • C. Only EU law applies because the data subjects are European.
  • D. The lower of the two regulatory burdens governs.

Correct: A Rationale: Data sovereignty — both jurisdictions can apply; cross-border transfer requires a lawful mechanism (e.g., Standard Contractual Clauses, adequacy decisions, BCRs). Tags: domain=2, difficulty=3, pattern=Legal, bloom=Apply

Q-D2-05 (difficulty 2, Remember, Definition)

Which control protects data in use, i.e., while it is being processed in memory?

  • A. TLS 1.3.
  • B. AES-256 disk encryption.
  • C. Trusted execution environments / secure enclaves (e.g., Intel SGX, AMD SEV) or memory-resident encryption.
  • D. SHA-256 hashing.

Correct: C Rationale: TLS protects data in transit; AES-at-rest covers storage; SHA-256 provides integrity but not confidentiality. Enclaves / encrypted memory address the in-use state. Tags: domain=2, difficulty=2, pattern=Definition, bloom=Remember

Q-D2-06 (difficulty 3, Apply, FIRST)

A company is rolling out a new data-classification programme. Which step should be done FIRST?

  • A. Buy a data-loss-prevention product.
  • B. Inventory and locate the data, including its owners and current uses.
  • C. Train staff on the new labels.
  • D. Encrypt every file in the file shares.

Correct: B Rationale: You cannot classify what you cannot find. Inventory and ownership precede labelling, controls, and training. Tags: domain=2, difficulty=3, pattern=FIRST, bloom=Apply

Q-D2-07 (difficulty 3, Analyze, MOST)

A regulator requires the company to retain financial records for seven years. The same records contain PII subject to a privacy regulation that mandates deletion when no longer needed. What is the MOST appropriate retention approach?

  • A. Delete the records as soon as the customer relationship ends.
  • B. Retain the records for seven years in the form required by the regulator, then dispose securely; minimise stored PII fields where feasible.
  • C. Retain the records indefinitely.
  • D. Anonymise the records on day one and rely on the anonymised copy for the seven-year obligation.

Correct: B Rationale: Regulatory minimum retention overrides the privacy as-soon-as-not-needed default. The data is still needed for the legal obligation. Data-minimisation principles allow reducing stored fields where the regulator does not require them. (D) risks failing the regulator's audit if the anonymisation removes attributes the regulator requires. Tags: domain=2, difficulty=3, pattern=MOST,Legal, bloom=Analyze

Q-D2-08 (difficulty 4, Evaluate, BEST)

A SaaS contract states that the SaaS vendor is the Data Processor and your company is the Data Controller. Which statement BEST describes accountability for the data?

  • A. The processor is accountable because they hold the data.
  • B. The controller is accountable; the processor is responsible for following the controller's lawful instructions, with the controller bearing ultimate accountability to data subjects and regulators.
  • C. Accountability is shared 50/50 by contract.
  • D. Whoever signs the SOC 2 attestation is accountable.

Correct: B Rationale: Under GDPR-style terminology, the controller decides why and how, and is accountable. The processor acts on instructions and has joint statutory obligations but is not the accountable party. Tags: domain=2, difficulty=4, pattern=BEST,Legal, bloom=Evaluate

Q-D2-09 (difficulty 2, Apply, Definition)

A backup tape of "Confidential" data is being transported off-site by a third-party courier. Which control is MOST important?

  • A. Bonded courier with chain-of-custody documentation, plus encryption of the tape's content.
  • B. Locked container only.
  • C. Tape encryption only.
  • D. Tracking GPS attached to the case.

Correct: A Rationale: Combine administrative (chain-of-custody) with technical (encryption). Encryption alone leaves an audit gap; physical-only controls leave a confidentiality gap. Tags: domain=2, difficulty=2, pattern=BEST, bloom=Apply

Q-D2-10 (difficulty 4, Analyze, MOST)

A research team requests access to production customer records to test a new ML model. What is the MOST appropriate response?

  • A. Grant access — the team needs realistic data.
  • B. Refuse access — production data must never be used.
  • C. Provide anonymised or synthetic data; if real data is unavoidable, use masking / tokenisation with documented business justification, owner approval, and time-bounded access.
  • D. Provide production data but require the team to sign an NDA.

Correct: C Rationale: Principle of least privilege + data minimisation. Anonymisation / synthesis preserves analytical value while reducing exposure. NDAs alone do not prevent breaches. Tags: domain=2, difficulty=4, pattern=MOST,Manager, bloom=Analyze

Domain 3 — Security Architecture and Engineering (13 %)

Pattern brief

Domain 3 is the models-and-math domain. Six archetypes:

  1. Security models — Bell-LaPadula (confidentiality: no read up / no write down), Biba (integrity: no read down / no write up), Clark-Wilson (well-formed transactions), Brewer-Nash (Chinese Wall, conflict-of-interest), Take-Grant, Graham-Denning.
  2. Evaluation criteria — Common Criteria EAL 1–7, Protection Profile, Security Target, Target of Evaluation. TCSEC / ITSEC are historic.
  3. Cryptography fundamentals — symmetric vs asymmetric, hashing vs encryption, key length cues, ECC vs RSA equivalents, perfect forward secrecy, digital signatures (sign with private, verify with public — and the message hash, not the message).
  4. Modes of operation — ECB never for confidential plaintext patterns; CBC needs IV; GCM provides confidentiality + integrity + AEAD.
  5. Embedded / industrial / IoT — defence-in-depth at the device + network + supplier level. ICS / SCADA prefer safety over confidentiality.
  6. Physical & site security — CPTED, fail-safe vs fail-secure (life safety = fail-safe / open; data = fail-secure / closed), fire-suppression class.

Wording cues: "confidentiality property" → BLP; "integrity property" → Biba; "conflict-of-interest" → Brewer-Nash; "well-formed transaction" → Clark-Wilson.

Q-D3-01 (difficulty 2, Remember, Definition)

The Bell-LaPadula Star (*) property states:

  • A. No read up.
  • B. No write down.
  • C. No read down.
  • D. No write up.

Correct: B Rationale: Star = "no write down" (high subject cannot write to a low object, preserving confidentiality). Simple security = "no read up". (C) / (D) are Biba properties. Tags: domain=3, difficulty=2, pattern=Definition, bloom=Remember

Q-D3-02 (difficulty 3, Understand, BEST)

For confidential data in transit, the BEST cipher mode is:

  • A. AES-ECB.
  • B. AES-CBC without an integrity tag.
  • C. AES-GCM (authenticated encryption with associated data).
  • D. RSA-2048 directly on the bulk payload.

Correct: C Rationale: AEAD = confidentiality + integrity in one primitive. ECB leaks plaintext patterns; CBC alone needs a MAC; RSA is for keys or signatures, not bulk encryption. Tags: domain=3, difficulty=3, pattern=BEST, bloom=Understand

Q-D3-03 (difficulty 3, Apply, Definition)

Alice signs a message and sends it to Bob. To verify the signature, Bob uses:

  • A. Alice's private key on the message.
  • B. Alice's public key on the hash of the message.
  • C. Bob's private key on the signed message.
  • D. A pre-shared symmetric key.

Correct: B Rationale: Signature = encrypted hash with the signer's private key; verification = decrypt the signature with the signer's public key and compare to a freshly computed hash of the received message. Tags: domain=3, difficulty=3, pattern=Definition, bloom=Apply

Q-D3-04 (difficulty 4, Analyze, MOST)

A data-centre is being designed to prioritise life safety of personnel during a fire. Door access controls should be configured as:

  • A. Fail-safe (doors unlock or open on power failure / alarm).
  • B. Fail-secure (doors lock on power failure).
  • C. Multi-factor on egress.
  • D. Magnetically locked in both states.

Correct: A Rationale: Life safety overrides confidentiality / integrity controls. Fail-safe = unlock on failure to allow egress. Fail-secure is for high-confidentiality areas where life-safety is otherwise handled. Tags: domain=3, difficulty=4, pattern=MOST, bloom=Analyze

Q-D3-05 (difficulty 3, Understand, Definition)

An HSM (Hardware Security Module) primarily provides which assurance?

  • A. Tamper-evident / tamper-resistant generation, storage, and use of cryptographic keys.
  • B. Faster TLS termination than software libraries.
  • C. End-user authentication via biometrics.
  • D. Free-standing time synchronisation.

Correct: A Rationale: HSMs centralise high-assurance key handling; speed is a side benefit; biometrics and time are unrelated. Tags: domain=3, difficulty=3, pattern=Definition, bloom=Understand

Q-D3-06 (difficulty 4, Apply, BEST)

A Common Criteria Protection Profile primarily defines:

  • A. A specific product's claimed security properties.
  • B. A set of security requirements for a class of products, written by users or regulators.
  • C. The vendor's marketing claims.
  • D. The penetration-testing methodology.

Correct: B Rationale: PP = requirements for a class (e.g., firewalls); ST = vendor's specific claim mapped to a PP; TOE = the actual product under evaluation. Tags: domain=3, difficulty=4, pattern=Definition, bloom=Apply

Q-D3-07 (difficulty 4, Evaluate, MOST)

An ICS / SCADA environment in a power plant exposes a legacy HMI to the IT network for ease of monitoring. The MOST critical risk is:

  • A. Loss of confidentiality of operator credentials.
  • B. Loss of safety / availability: a compromise can cause physical damage or unsafe states.
  • C. Loss of integrity of historical trend data.
  • D. Slow response time on the HMI.

Correct: B Rationale: ICS / OT prioritises safety then availability then integrity then confidentiality — the inverse of the standard IT CIA priority. Tags: domain=3, difficulty=4, pattern=MOST, bloom=Evaluate

Q-D3-08 (difficulty 3, Apply, FIRST)

You discover that an internal application is using AES-ECB to encrypt customer profile photos stored in object storage. What should be done FIRST?

  • A. Switch to AES-GCM and re-encrypt — ECB leaks block-level patterns that can disclose image content.
  • B. Increase the key length from 128 to 256 bits.
  • C. Add TLS to the object-storage API.
  • D. Move the data to a different cloud region.

Correct: A Rationale: The visible failure mode (the famous ECB-penguin) is the immediate risk. Key length is irrelevant; TLS protects in transit, not at rest; region change is unrelated. Tags: domain=3, difficulty=3, pattern=FIRST, bloom=Apply

Q-D3-09 (difficulty 2, Remember, Definition)

Perfect Forward Secrecy ensures that:

  • A. The long-term private key is destroyed after each session.
  • B. Compromise of the long-term private key does not allow decryption of past session keys, because session keys are derived from ephemeral key-exchange material.
  • C. The cipher is mathematically unbreakable.
  • D. Both endpoints must rotate certificates daily.

Correct: B Rationale: Ephemeral Diffie-Hellman (DHE / ECDHE) provides PFS by ensuring session keys are not derivable from the long-term key alone. Tags: domain=3, difficulty=2, pattern=Definition, bloom=Remember

Q-D3-10 (difficulty 4, Analyze, BEST)

A company is choosing a cryptographic primitive for secure firmware update verification on a constrained IoT device. The BEST choice is:

  • A. Symmetric AES-CBC with a device-stored key.
  • B. Digital signature using ECDSA (or Ed25519) — the device holds the manufacturer's public key.
  • C. SHA-256 hash of the firmware embedded in the image.
  • D. HMAC-SHA256 with a shared secret stored on every device.

Correct: B Rationale: Asymmetric verification means the device never needs a secret; signing key stays with the manufacturer. (A) requires distributing a confidentiality-grade secret to every device; (C) provides no authenticity; (D) compromises every device if one secret leaks. Tags: domain=3, difficulty=4, pattern=BEST, bloom=Analyze

Domain 4 — Communication and Network Security (13 %)

Pattern brief

Domain 4 is the layer-and-protocol domain. Six archetypes:

  1. OSI / TCP-IP layers — know which protocol lives where and which attack targets which layer. ARP spoofing → L2; routing attacks → L3; SYN flood → L4; injection → L7.
  2. VPN selection — site-to-site IPsec for network-to-network; SSL/TLS VPN for user remote access; WireGuard as modern alternative.
  3. Wireless — WPA3-SAE preferred, WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X / EAP-TLS) for managed estates, WPA2-PSK / WPA / WEP only in narrowly justified legacy contexts.
  4. Segmentation — VLANs are not a security boundary on their own; combine with firewalls / ACLs. Zero Trust deprecates network-perimeter trust.
  5. DNS / TLS — DNSSEC for integrity of resolution; DoT / DoH for confidentiality. TLS 1.3 minimum; deprecate SSLv3 / TLS 1.0 / 1.1.
  6. NAC / IDS / IPS / proxies / load balancers — know placement order: filtering → inspection → load distribution → application.

Wording cues: "across an untrusted network" → encrypted tunnel; "from internet to DMZ" → ingress firewall + IPS; "between hosts in the same broadcast domain" → segmentation gap.

Q-D4-01 (difficulty 2, Remember, Definition)

A SYN-flood attack targets which OSI layer?

  • A. Layer 2 (Data Link).
  • B. Layer 3 (Network).
  • C. Layer 4 (Transport).
  • D. Layer 7 (Application).

Correct: C Rationale: SYN exploits the TCP three-way handshake at the transport layer. Tags: domain=4, difficulty=2, pattern=Definition, bloom=Remember

Q-D4-02 (difficulty 3, Apply, BEST)

The BEST wireless authentication mode for a corporate office of 5,000 employees with managed devices is:

  • A. WPA2-PSK with a strong shared passphrase rotated annually.
  • B. WPA2 / WPA3 Enterprise with 802.1X and EAP-TLS using device certificates.
  • C. Open SSID with a captive-portal sign-in.
  • D. MAC-address filtering only.

Correct: B Rationale: Per-user / per-device credentials, mutual auth via certificates, automatic key derivation. PSK shares secrets across users; MAC filtering is trivially bypassed. Tags: domain=4, difficulty=3, pattern=BEST, bloom=Apply

Q-D4-03 (difficulty 3, Apply, FIRST)

An analyst observes a sustained ARP-spoofing pattern on an internal subnet. What should be done FIRST?

  • A. Reimage all hosts on the subnet.
  • B. Isolate the subnet / disable the offending switch port and validate the legitimate gateway MAC.
  • C. Block all outbound traffic at the perimeter firewall.
  • D. Notify the data-protection officer.

Correct: B Rationale: Containment of an L2 attack happens at L2. Reimaging or perimeter blocking is over- or under-targeted. Tags: domain=4, difficulty=3, pattern=FIRST, bloom=Apply

Q-D4-04 (difficulty 3, Understand, Definition)

DNSSEC primarily provides:

  • A. Confidentiality of DNS queries.
  • B. Origin authentication and integrity of DNS responses via digital signatures.
  • C. Availability of DNS resolvers under DDoS.
  • D. Encryption of zone transfers between primary and secondary.

Correct: B Rationale: DNSSEC signs records so resolvers can verify origin and integrity. Confidentiality is provided by DoT / DoH; availability is a separate concern. Tags: domain=4, difficulty=3, pattern=Definition, bloom=Understand

Q-D4-05 (difficulty 4, Analyze, MOST)

Two business units have hosts in the same VLAN. A compliance auditor notes that the units handle data of different classifications. The MOST appropriate response is:

  • A. Add a sign on the rack warning users to be careful.
  • B. Implement L3 segmentation with a firewall or microsegmentation between the units; VLANs alone are administrative, not a security boundary.
  • C. Encrypt all data at rest on every host.
  • D. Move both units to the public cloud.

Correct: B Rationale: VLAN ≠ security boundary. Segmentation needs an inspecting / filtering chokepoint. Tags: domain=4, difficulty=4, pattern=MOST, bloom=Analyze

Q-D4-06 (difficulty 3, Apply, BEST)

Which VPN type is BEST for remote user access from variable, untrusted networks?

  • A. Site-to-site IPsec tunnel.
  • B. TLS / SSL VPN with strong client authentication.
  • C. Generic Routing Encapsulation (GRE) tunnel.
  • D. L2TP without IPsec.

Correct: B Rationale: TLS VPN traverses common firewall / NAT environments cleanly and supports per-user policy. IPsec site-to-site is for network-to-network; GRE is not confidential; L2TP without IPsec is not confidential. Tags: domain=4, difficulty=3, pattern=BEST, bloom=Apply

Q-D4-07 (difficulty 3, Apply, FIRST)

A web application is being moved from on-prem to a cloud provider. The architecture team is debating whether to keep an on-prem-style perimeter firewall. The FIRST step is:

  • A. Buy the largest cloud-firewall appliance available.
  • B. Define the trust boundaries and required flows, then pick controls (cloud firewalls, security groups, WAF, service mesh) to enforce them — perimeter alone is insufficient in cloud.
  • C. Disable all logging to reduce noise.
  • D. Place every workload in a single VPC for simplicity.

Correct: B Rationale: Cloud security follows the identity- and flow-aware model; trust-boundary definition precedes control selection. Tags: domain=4, difficulty=3, pattern=FIRST, bloom=Apply

Q-D4-08 (difficulty 2, Remember, Definition)

The MOST appropriate protocol for protecting confidentiality of web traffic today is:

  • A. SSL 3.0.
  • B. TLS 1.0.
  • C. TLS 1.1.
  • D. TLS 1.3.

Correct: D Rationale: TLS 1.3 is current; SSL 3.0, TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1 are deprecated by major regulators and browsers. Tags: domain=4, difficulty=2, pattern=Definition, bloom=Remember

Q-D4-09 (difficulty 4, Analyze, BEST)

A network team wants to move toward Zero Trust. Which principle is MOST aligned with that goal?

  • A. Build a stronger perimeter firewall.
  • B. Never trust, always verify — authenticate and authorise every request based on identity, device, and context, regardless of network location.
  • C. Place all internal services on the same flat network.
  • D. Disable multi-factor authentication for internal users to reduce friction.

Correct: B Rationale: Zero Trust replaces perimeter trust with continuous verification per-request. Tags: domain=4, difficulty=4, pattern=BEST, bloom=Analyze

Q-D4-10 (difficulty 4, Evaluate, MOST)

The MOST significant risk of using a transparent web proxy without TLS interception in a corporate environment is:

  • A. Slow page loads.
  • B. Limited visibility into HTTPS-tunnelled threats (e.g., data exfiltration, malware C2 over HTTPS).
  • C. Inability to cache JavaScript.
  • D. Loss of geographic routing.

Correct: B Rationale: Without inspection of the encrypted payload, content-aware controls (DLP, AV, URL category beyond SNI) are blind. TLS interception is a privacy and compliance decision, not a free win. Tags: domain=4, difficulty=4, pattern=MOST, bloom=Evaluate

Domain 5 — Identity and Access Management (13 %)

Pattern brief

Domain 5 is the lifecycle-and-federation domain. Six archetypes:

  1. IAAA — Identification (who you claim to be) → Authentication (proof) → Authorisation (what you can do) → Accountability (logged & traceable).
  2. MFA factors — know (password / PIN), have (token / smartcard / phone), be (biometric), do (behavioural), where (location). Two factors must be from different categories.
  3. Access-control models — DAC (owner discretion), MAC (system-enforced labels), RBAC (roles), ABAC (attributes / policy), Rule-based, Risk-based / adaptive.
  4. Federation protocols — SAML 2.0 (XML, enterprise SSO), OAuth 2.0 (authorisation delegation), OIDC (OAuth + identity layer), Kerberos (intra-realm, ticket-based).
  5. JML lifecycle — Joiner / Mover / Leaver. Leaver is the most exam-loved trick (de-provision on day-zero, recover the data, not the badge alone).
  6. Privileged-access management — vault, session recording, just-in-time elevation, break-glass, separation of duties for admin pairs.

Wording cues: "across organisations" → SAML / OIDC; "delegate third-party app access" → OAuth; "user logs in once and gets to many internal apps" → SSO (Kerberos or SAML); "lost laptop" → revoke certificate + tokens, not just password.

Q-D5-01 (difficulty 2, Remember, Definition)

Authorisation answers which question?

  • A. Who is this user?
  • B. Can this user prove their identity?
  • C. What is this authenticated user allowed to do?
  • D. Was this user's action recorded?

Correct: C Rationale: Authorisation = permissions / rights of an already-authenticated principal. (A) identification; (B) authentication; (D) accountability. Tags: domain=5, difficulty=2, pattern=Definition, bloom=Remember

Q-D5-02 (difficulty 3, Apply, BEST)

A web app must let users sign in with their personal Google account and then call a third-party calendar API on the user's behalf. The BEST protocol mix is:

  • A. SAML 2.0 only.
  • B. OIDC for authentication + OAuth 2.0 for authorisation to the calendar API.
  • C. Kerberos.
  • D. Direct LDAP bind to Google.

Correct: B Rationale: OIDC delivers an ID token (who); OAuth delivers an access token (what); the two together are the modern consumer-federation pattern. Tags: domain=5, difficulty=3, pattern=BEST, bloom=Apply

Q-D5-03 (difficulty 3, Apply, FIRST)

An employee resigns on a Friday afternoon and walks out. The FIRST identity-management action on Friday evening is:

  • A. Schedule a HR exit interview.
  • B. Disable all of the user's authentication credentials (account lock, certificate revocation, MFA token de-bind, SSO sessions terminated).
  • C. Archive the user's email PST file.
  • D. Recover the corporate laptop.

Correct: B Rationale: Time-to-revoke is the live access risk. Asset recovery and data archiving follow. Tags: domain=5, difficulty=3, pattern=FIRST, bloom=Apply

Q-D5-04 (difficulty 2, Understand, Definition)

Two factors must be from different categories to count as MFA. Which pair below is NOT true MFA?

  • A. Password + hardware token.
  • B. Password + fingerprint.
  • C. Password + security question (both "know").
  • D. Smartcard + PIN.

Correct: C Rationale: Two "know" factors are both knowledge — same category. Real MFA crosses know / have / be. Tags: domain=5, difficulty=2, pattern=Definition, bloom=Understand

Q-D5-05 (difficulty 3, Apply, BEST)

For privileged-administrator access in a Tier-0 directory, the BEST control set includes:

  • A. Strong passwords rotated every 30 days.
  • B. Privileged-access vault, just-in-time elevation, MFA, session recording, jump-host, separation of duties, periodic recert.
  • C. Shared admin account with logging.
  • D. Long-lived admin SSH keys committed to source control.

Correct: B Rationale: PAM stack covers identity, time-boxing, evidence, and accountability. (A) is necessary but not sufficient; (C) and (D) violate accountability and least-privilege. Tags: domain=5, difficulty=3, pattern=BEST, bloom=Apply

Q-D5-06 (difficulty 4, Analyze, MOST)

An employee transfers from finance to engineering. Three months later, an audit shows the user still has finance application access. Which JML failure is MOST likely?

  • A. Joiner.
  • B. Mover (entitlements were added at the new role but not removed for the old role).
  • C. Leaver.
  • D. None — three months of overlap is normal.

Correct: B Rationale: Movers accrue privilege creep when only "add" tickets fire on transfer. Periodic entitlement reviews catch this; ideal is automatic reconciliation against the user's current role. Tags: domain=5, difficulty=4, pattern=MOST,Manager, bloom=Analyze

Q-D5-07 (difficulty 3, Understand, Definition)

ABAC differs from RBAC primarily because:

  • A. ABAC is older.
  • B. ABAC evaluates attributes (user attributes, resource attributes, environment) via a policy, allowing fine-grained, context-aware decisions; RBAC is role-table-driven.
  • C. ABAC does not require authentication.
  • D. RBAC supports MFA; ABAC does not.

Correct: B Rationale: ABAC = policy + attributes (XACML, OPA). RBAC = role tables. Tags: domain=5, difficulty=3, pattern=Definition, bloom=Understand

Q-D5-08 (difficulty 4, Evaluate, BEST)

A SaaS application accepts a SAML assertion from your organisation's IdP. Which signing / validation property is MOST important?

  • A. The assertion is encrypted with AES-128-GCM.
  • B. The SP validates the IdP's XML signature on the assertion and checks NotBefore / NotOnOrAfter / audience / recipient.
  • C. The IdP and SP exchange a shared password monthly.
  • D. The browser caches the assertion locally.

Correct: B Rationale: SAML's integrity model is signature-based; replay protection comes from time and audience checks. Encryption of the assertion is sometimes used but is not the central trust mechanic. Tags: domain=5, difficulty=4, pattern=BEST, bloom=Evaluate

Q-D5-09 (difficulty 3, Apply, FIRST)

A user reports their phone (the MFA "have" factor) was stolen. The FIRST action is:

  • A. Buy them a new phone.
  • B. Revoke the lost factor in the IdP (de-bind the TOTP / push token), require re-enrolment of a new factor.
