  • C. Lower the MFA requirement to password only until the user gets a new phone.
  • D. Email the user a temporary password.

Correct: B Rationale: Treat as a credential-theft incident — revoke the compromised factor first. Tags: domain=5, difficulty=3, pattern=FIRST, bloom=Apply

Q-D5-10 (difficulty 4, Analyze, MOST)

A long-running batch service runs as a shared service account with a static password embedded in scripts. The MOST significant control gap is:

  • A. The password is short.
  • B. Loss of accountability and inability to rotate without coordinated downtime — recommend a managed-identity / vaulted credential with auditable retrieval.
  • C. The service runs at night.
  • D. The script is in version control.

Correct: B Rationale: Shared static credentials break IAAA's accountability leg; modern fix is workload identity (e.g., SPIFFE, cloud managed identities) or a brokered secret with audit. Tags: domain=5, difficulty=4, pattern=MOST,Manager, bloom=Analyze

Domain 6 — Security Assessment and Testing (12 %)

Pattern brief

Domain 6 is the assurance domain. Five archetypes:

  1. Audit types — internal (own staff), external / independent (third-party), regulatory. SOC 1 = financial controls; SOC 2 = trust services criteria (security, availability, confidentiality, processing integrity, privacy); SOC 3 = public summary. Type I = design at a point in time; Type II = operating effectiveness over a period.
  2. Vulnerability vs penetration testing — vuln scan is breadth (automated, list of issues, no exploitation); pen test is depth (controlled exploitation, ROE-bound, validated impact).
  3. Test design — black-box (no internal info), grey-box (some info), white-box / crystal-box (full info, fastest).
  4. Code-level testing — SAST (static), DAST (dynamic), IAST (instrumented), SCA (third-party libraries), fuzzing.
  5. KPIs / KRIs — MTTD, MTTR, % patched within SLA, % MFA coverage, false-positive rate, audit-finding closure rate. Metrics must roll up to risk and to business outcomes.

Wording cues: "before going to production" → SAST + SCA + DAST in pipeline; "after a major release" → pen test; "for our customers" → SOC 2 Type II report; "to our auditors" → internal-audit attestation, not pen test.

Q-D6-01 (difficulty 2, Remember, Definition)

A SOC 2 Type II report covers:

  • A. The financial controls of the audited organisation.
  • B. The design of the audited organisation's controls at a single point in time.
  • C. The design and operating effectiveness of the audited organisation's controls over the trust services criteria across a defined period (typically 6–12 months).
  • D. A public-relations summary of the organisation's security posture.

Correct: C Rationale: Type I = design (point in time). Type II = design + operating effectiveness (period). SOC 1 = ICFR (financial). SOC 3 = public. Tags: domain=6, difficulty=2, pattern=Definition, bloom=Remember

Q-D6-02 (difficulty 3, Apply, BEST)

The BEST testing strategy to detect a buffer-overflow in a custom C++ service is:

  • A. SAST plus targeted fuzzing against the parser inputs.
  • B. SCA for the third-party libraries used by the service.
  • C. SOC 2 evidence collection.
  • D. Tabletop exercise with the dev team.

Correct: A Rationale: Buffer overflows hide in code logic and untested input paths — SAST + fuzz is the sweet spot. Tags: domain=6, difficulty=3, pattern=BEST, bloom=Apply

Q-D6-03 (difficulty 3, Understand, Definition)

Black-box penetration testing means:

  • A. The tester has full source-code access.
  • B. The tester has no prior internal knowledge and approaches the system like an external attacker.
  • C. The tester is paid in cryptocurrency.
  • D. The engagement uses no automated tools.

Correct: B Rationale: Black-box ≈ external attacker view. Grey-box ≈ partial. White-box / crystal-box ≈ full. Tags: domain=6, difficulty=3, pattern=Definition, bloom=Understand

Q-D6-04 (difficulty 4, Analyze, FIRST)

A pen-test report lists 50 findings. The FIRST step the security team should take is:

  • A. Email every owner asking them to fix everything by Friday.
  • B. Triage by exploitability, blast radius, and business impact, then prioritise remediation with owners.
  • C. Re-test all findings immediately.
  • D. Publish the report on the company intranet.

Correct: B Rationale: Volume without priority causes whiplash. Triage maps findings to owners and SLAs. Tags: domain=6, difficulty=4, pattern=FIRST,Manager, bloom=Analyze

Q-D6-05 (difficulty 3, Apply, BEST)

The BEST way to verify whether a software supply-chain library has known vulnerabilities is:

  • A. Manual code review.
  • B. Software Composition Analysis (SCA) against the dependency manifest with a current CVE feed.
  • C. DAST against the deployed service.
  • D. Pen test of the production environment.

Correct: B Rationale: SCA enumerates third-party components and matches against vulnerability data. SAST / DAST find logic issues; pen test confirms exploitability but is downstream. Tags: domain=6, difficulty=3, pattern=BEST, bloom=Apply

Q-D6-06 (difficulty 4, Evaluate, MOST)

The MOST important attribute of a security metric reported to the board is:

  • A. It contains many decimal places.
  • B. It is tied to a business outcome or risk, has a defined target, trend, and owner, and is comparable period-over-period.
  • C. It uses CVSS as the only scale.
  • D. It is generated automatically with no human interpretation.

Correct: B Rationale: Boards want signal (direction, ownership, target) not raw output. CVSS alone misses business context. Tags: domain=6, difficulty=4, pattern=MOST,Manager, bloom=Evaluate

Q-D6-07 (difficulty 3, Apply, FIRST)

Your CI pipeline is being designed to catch security issues. Which testing stage runs earliest in the pipeline?

  • A. DAST.
  • B. SAST + SCA on every commit.
  • C. Production pen test.
  • D. Customer SOC 2 audit.

Correct: B Rationale: Shift-left: static / composition analysis run at build time before any artefact exists to deploy. DAST follows in a test environment. Tags: domain=6, difficulty=3, pattern=FIRST, bloom=Apply

Q-D6-08 (difficulty 3, Apply, BEST)

A regulator demands evidence that user-access reviews are happening every quarter. The BEST evidence is:

  • A. A screenshot of the IdP console showing all users.
  • B. Signed attestations from line managers for each application, with the underlying entitlement extract, exception log, and remediation tickets.
  • C. A vendor's marketing brochure.
  • D. The CISO's verbal assurance.

Correct: B Rationale: Auditable evidence = artefact + signature + traceability of exceptions. Tags: domain=6, difficulty=3, pattern=BEST, bloom=Apply

Q-D6-09 (difficulty 4, Analyze, MOST)

Penetration-test Rules of Engagement (ROE) MOST importantly define:

  • A. The pen tester's daily rate.
  • B. Scope (in / out), permitted techniques, escalation paths, communication channels, working hours, and emergency stop conditions, plus written authorisation.
  • C. The exact CVEs to be tested.
  • D. The colour of the report cover.

Correct: B Rationale: ROE is what makes the testing lawful, safe, and useful. (A) belongs in the MSA, (C) is overly prescriptive, (D) is humour. Tags: domain=6, difficulty=4, pattern=MOST, bloom=Analyze

Q-D6-10 (difficulty 4, Evaluate, BEST)

A vulnerability scanner reports 2,300 findings. Roughly 40 % are duplicates and 30 % are false positives. The BEST next action is:

  • A. Patch every reported finding regardless.
  • B. De-duplicate, validate, prioritise, and tune the scanner; track time-to-remediate against severity-based SLAs.
  • C. Switch to a different scanner immediately.
  • D. Disable the scanner — too noisy.

Correct: B Rationale: Operational hygiene: clean the signal, then act. False-positive tuning is itself a metric. Tags: domain=6, difficulty=4, pattern=BEST,Manager, bloom=Evaluate

Domain 7 — Security Operations (13 %)

Pattern brief

Domain 7 is the run-and-respond domain. Six archetypes:

  1. Incident-response lifecycle — Prepare → Detect → Contain → Eradicate → Recover → Lessons learned. The biggest exam trap is reordering containment and eradication; you stop the bleeding before you treat the wound.
  2. Forensics — chain of custody, integrity hashing of evidence, write-blockers, volatile-data order (RFC 3227: registers → memory → temp → disk → archives).
  3. BCP / DRP — BIA → RTO / RPO / MTD → strategy → test → maintain. RTO is recovery time; RPO is acceptable data loss; MTD is the absolute upper bound.
  4. Backup strategy — 3-2-1 rule (3 copies, 2 media, 1 off-site); test restores; protect backups from ransomware (offline / immutable).
  5. Logging / SIEM / SOC — centralised, time-synced (NTP), tamper-evident, retention as per policy + regulation. Detection use-cases tied to MITRE ATT&CK.
  6. Change & config management — emergency changes have a back-out plan; CAB approval; baseline + drift detection.

Wording cues: "FIRST during incident" → Contain (almost always). "Before declaring incident over" → Lessons learned + evidence preservation. "Restore service" → RTO; "Data lost" → RPO.

Q-D7-01 (difficulty 2, Remember, Definition)

The correct order of the incident-response lifecycle is:

  • A. Detect → Eradicate → Contain → Recover.
  • B. Prepare → Detect → Contain → Eradicate → Recover → Lessons Learned.
  • C. Contain → Prepare → Detect → Eradicate → Recover.
  • D. Detect → Recover → Contain → Eradicate.

Correct: B Rationale: Containment before eradication is the most-tested ordering. Lessons-learned closes the loop. Tags: domain=7, difficulty=2, pattern=Definition, bloom=Remember

Q-D7-02 (difficulty 3, Apply, FIRST)

You confirm an active ransomware encryption process on a file server. What should be done FIRST?

  • A. Open a ticket with the antivirus vendor.
  • B. Isolate the host from the network (disable network interface, pull the cable, or quarantine via EDR).
  • C. Image the disk for forensics.
  • D. Restore from backup over the encryption.

Correct: B Rationale: Containment first stops the bleeding. Forensics and recovery follow. Restoring over the encryption risks losing forensic evidence and re-encrypting if the threat is still active. Tags: domain=7, difficulty=3, pattern=FIRST, bloom=Apply

Q-D7-03 (difficulty 3, Understand, Definition)

RPO stands for:

  • A. Recovery Plan Owner.
  • B. Recovery Point Objective — the maximum tolerable amount of data loss measured in time.
  • C. Required Patch Order.
  • D. Risk Probability Outcome.

Correct: B Rationale: RPO = acceptable data-loss horizon. Paired with RTO (recovery time) and MTD (max tolerable downtime). Tags: domain=7, difficulty=3, pattern=Definition, bloom=Understand

Q-D7-04 (difficulty 4, Apply, BEST)

A critical system has an MTD of 4 hours. Replication adds cost. The BEST recovery strategy is:

  • A. Cold site, weekly tape restores.
  • B. Warm or hot site with replication tuned so RTO ≤ MTD and RPO matches business tolerance, validated by annual recovery testing.
  • C. No DR; rely on cloud auto-healing.
  • D. Document a plan but do not test it.

Correct: B Rationale: Strategy must satisfy MTD and be exercised. Cold site rarely meets a 4 h MTD; auto-healing alone is not assurance. Tags: domain=7, difficulty=4, pattern=BEST, bloom=Apply

Q-D7-05 (difficulty 3, Apply, FIRST)

The FIRST step in a forensic investigation of a suspected compromise is:

  • A. Reboot the host to clear it.
  • B. Preserve volatile data and disk images using validated tools, document chain of custody from the moment of contact.
  • C. Run a full antivirus scan in-place.
  • D. Tell the suspect employee about the investigation.

Correct: B Rationale: Volatile data (memory, network connections) disappears on reboot. Chain of custody begins at first contact. Tags: domain=7, difficulty=3, pattern=FIRST, bloom=Apply

Q-D7-06 (difficulty 3, Understand, Definition)

The 3-2-1 backup rule says:

  • A. 3 backups per year.
  • B. 3 copies, 2 different media, 1 off-site (or offline / immutable).
  • C. 3 administrators, 2 sites, 1 vault.
  • D. 3 weeks of retention.

Correct: B Rationale: 3-2-1 covers single-media failure, single-site disaster, and (modernised) ransomware via offline / immutable copy. Tags: domain=7, difficulty=3, pattern=Definition, bloom=Understand

Q-D7-07 (difficulty 4, Analyze, MOST)

A SOC is overwhelmed by alerts. Tier-1 analysts spend 80 % of their day on false positives. The MOST impactful improvement is:

  • A. Hire more analysts.
  • B. Tune detection rules with feedback loops, suppress noisy signatures, automate enrichment, and align use-cases to MITRE ATT&CK with measured value.
  • C. Switch SIEM vendors.
  • D. Disable detections that cause false positives.

Correct: B Rationale: False-positive reduction is an engineering exercise — content quality, enrichment, automation — not headcount or rip-and-replace. Tags: domain=7, difficulty=4, pattern=MOST,Manager, bloom=Analyze

Q-D7-08 (difficulty 4, Evaluate, BEST)

Backups are stored on the same SAN as production. The BEST mitigation against ransomware is:

  • A. Increase backup frequency.
  • B. Add an offline / immutable copy (air-gapped tape, immutable object lock) and test restore regularly.
  • C. Encrypt the backups in place.
  • D. Move backups to a different folder on the SAN.

Correct: B Rationale: Same-SAN backups are co-encrypted by the ransomware. Immutability and offline / out-of-band copies break the blast radius. Tags: domain=7, difficulty=4, pattern=BEST, bloom=Evaluate

Q-D7-09 (difficulty 3, Apply, FIRST)

A production outage has begun. The FIRST step in the change-management response is:

  • A. Wait for the next CAB meeting.
  • B. Invoke the emergency change procedure: declare incident, identify the most likely change to back out, follow the documented emergency path with appropriate approvals, then post-review.
  • C. Restart every server.
  • D. Roll forward with untested fixes.

Correct: B Rationale: Emergency change processes exist specifically for this — fast but accountable. Tags: domain=7, difficulty=3, pattern=FIRST,Manager, bloom=Apply

Q-D7-10 (difficulty 4, Analyze, MOST)

A post-incident review reveals the same root cause has produced four incidents in 18 months. The MOST important deliverable from this review is:

  • A. A new dashboard.
  • B. A corrective-action plan with named owners, dates, and a verification step that closes the root cause, tracked to completion.
  • C. A blame report on the engineering team.
  • D. A press release.

Correct: B Rationale: Repeat-incident pattern means systemic weakness — corrective actions and verification, not optics. Tags: domain=7, difficulty=4, pattern=MOST,Manager, bloom=Analyze

Domain 8 — Software Development Security (10 %)

Pattern brief

Domain 8 is the secure-SDLC domain. Five archetypes:

  1. Secure SDLC — security in every phase: requirements (threat modelling), design (architecture review), implement (secure coding + SAST + peer review), test (DAST / IAST / fuzzing / pen test), deploy (config hardening), maintain (patching + monitoring).
  2. OWASP Top 10 — injection (parameterised queries), broken auth, sensitive-data exposure, XXE, broken access control, security misconfig, XSS, insecure deserialisation, vulnerable components, insufficient logging.
  3. Threat modelling — STRIDE (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Info disclosure, DoS, Elevation), PASTA, DREAD; data-flow diagrams to find trust boundaries.
  4. DevSecOps — pipeline-integrated SAST / SCA / DAST, container scanning, IaC scanning, secrets scanning, build provenance / SBOM.
  5. API security — authn (OAuth tokens / mTLS), authz (per-resource), rate-limit, schema validation, output filtering. Avoid mass assignment, IDOR, BOLA.

Wording cues: "stored procedures are an alternative to …" → SQL injection prevention discussion. "uses unvetted user input as code" → injection. "trust boundary" → threat-model artefact.

Q-D8-01 (difficulty 2, Apply, BEST)

A new search feature concatenates user input into a SQL query. The BEST control is:

  • A. Validate the input length.
  • B. Parameterised queries / prepared statements with bound variables; combine with least-privilege DB account.
  • C. Add a WAF in front of the application.
  • D. Hash the user input before insertion.

Correct: B Rationale: Parameterisation separates code from data at the SQL boundary, eliminating injection at source. WAF is defence-in-depth, not a substitute. Tags: domain=8, difficulty=2, pattern=BEST, bloom=Apply

Q-D8-02 (difficulty 3, Apply, FIRST)

The FIRST activity in secure design of a new microservice is:

  • A. Pick a programming language.
  • B. Threat-model the service (data-flow diagram, trust boundaries, STRIDE per element).
  • C. Provision the production database.
  • D. Write the marketing copy.

Correct: B Rationale: Threat modelling at design time produces requirements that cost an order of magnitude less to address than at test or production. Tags: domain=8, difficulty=3, pattern=FIRST, bloom=Apply

Q-D8-03 (difficulty 3, Understand, Definition)

STRIDE is a threat-modelling mnemonic for:

  • A. Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege.
  • B. Secrets, TLS, Roles, Identity, Data, Encryption.
  • C. SQL, Tokens, RBAC, Identity, Devices, Endpoint.
  • D. Sandboxing, Threats, Risk, Impact, Damage, Exposure.

Correct: A Rationale: Memorise STRIDE; classify each DFD element against the six categories. Tags: domain=8, difficulty=3, pattern=Definition, bloom=Understand

Q-D8-04 (difficulty 4, Analyze, MOST)

An API endpoint returns a customer record when a numeric customerId is supplied. Any authenticated user can request any customerId and receive the data. The MOST accurate vulnerability description is:

  • A. SQL injection.
  • B. Broken Object-Level Authorisation (BOLA / IDOR) — the server fails to verify the requester's authorisation for the requested object.
  • C. Cross-site scripting.
  • D. XML external entity.

Correct: B Rationale: BOLA / IDOR is the API-era version of "you can change the id in the URL". Fix is per-resource authz checks, not just authentication. Tags: domain=8, difficulty=4, pattern=MOST, bloom=Analyze

Q-D8-05 (difficulty 3, Apply, BEST)

The BEST defence against cross-site scripting (XSS) is:

  • A. Server-side input filtering by removing < and >.
  • B. Context-aware output encoding (HTML, attribute, JS, CSS, URL encoding depending on sink), plus a strong Content-Security-Policy, plus framework-default escaping.
  • C. Disable JavaScript in user browsers.
  • D. Inspect outgoing responses with a WAF.

Correct: B Rationale: XSS is an output problem (data interpreted as code by a browser). Encoding at the sink + CSP is the canonical defence. Tags: domain=8, difficulty=3, pattern=BEST, bloom=Apply

Q-D8-06 (difficulty 3, Apply, FIRST)

Before deploying a container image to production, the FIRST automated security gate is:

  • A. Buy a runtime EDR.
  • B. Vulnerability scan of the image (OS packages + app dependencies) plus signature / provenance check (SBOM, signed by build system).
  • C. Live pen-test the production cluster.
  • D. Email the security team.

Correct: B Rationale: Pre-deploy image scan + provenance ensures only known-good, attested artefacts run. Tags: domain=8, difficulty=3, pattern=FIRST, bloom=Apply

Q-D8-07 (difficulty 4, Evaluate, BEST)

A third-party JavaScript library is imported via CDN with <script src="https://cdn.example.invalid/lib.js">. The BEST integrity control is:

  • A. Switch the script tag to http.
  • B. Subresource Integrity (SRI) — pin a SHA-384 hash of the library to the tag, plus a Content-Security-Policy that restricts script sources.
  • C. Copy the script into the project source and forget about it.
  • D. Trust the CDN's TLS certificate alone.

Correct: B Rationale: SRI lets the browser verify the fetched content against a pinned hash; TLS alone trusts the CDN provider's whole supply chain. Tags: domain=8, difficulty=4, pattern=BEST, bloom=Evaluate

Q-D8-08 (difficulty 3, Analyze, MOST)

A new microservice stores customer documents in object storage. The bucket is public-read by default in the cloud account. The MOST significant risk is:

  • A. Higher storage cost.
  • B. Mass data exposure due to security misconfiguration — public buckets are a leading cause of cloud breaches.
  • C. Slow downloads.
  • D. SEO penalty.

Correct: B Rationale: Bucket misconfiguration is OWASP API top-10 / cloud top-risk material; immediate fix is private-by-default with explicit grants. Tags: domain=8, difficulty=3, pattern=MOST, bloom=Analyze

Q-D8-09 (difficulty 4, Apply, BEST)

A monolithic application is being refactored into microservices. To prevent secrets sprawl, the BEST practice is:

  • A. Email developers a master .env file.
  • B. Use a centralised secret-management service with workload identity, short-lived credentials, audit logging, and rotation; never store secrets in source.
  • C. Hard-code secrets in environment files committed to git.
  • D. Encrypt secrets with a passphrase shared on Slack.

Correct: B Rationale: Secret-manager + workload identity (e.g., SPIFFE, cloud-IAM-issued tokens) provides ephemeral, audited credentials. Tags: domain=8, difficulty=4, pattern=BEST,Manager, bloom=Apply

Q-D8-10 (difficulty 4, Evaluate, MOST)

A development team is asked to "add security to the pipeline". They propose running a manual pen test once a quarter and nothing else. The MOST appropriate executive response is:

  • A. Approve — quarterly pen testing is sufficient.
  • B. Reject — propose a defence-in-depth shift-left: SAST / SCA / secrets scanning on commit, container & IaC scans on build, DAST and integration tests on deploy, plus periodic pen tests and threat modelling at design; pen tests alone are sparse, late, and expensive.
  • C. Reject — only adopt DAST.
  • D. Approve and reduce frequency to annual.

Correct: B Rationale: Pen tests are episodic and downstream; shift-left + multi-layer scanning catches issues at the cheapest moment. Tags: domain=8, difficulty=4, pattern=MOST,Manager, bloom=Evaluate

Cross-domain coverage summary

DomainPattern types coveredDifficulty spanBloom span
D1 — Risk & GovernanceCalculation, BEST, FIRST, MOST, Manager, Legal, Ethics, Definition2–4Remember → Evaluate
D2 — Asset SecurityDefinition, Manager, BEST, Legal, FIRST, MOST2–4Remember → Evaluate
D3 — Architecture & EngineeringDefinition, BEST, MOST, FIRST2–4Remember → Analyze
D4 — Network SecurityDefinition, BEST, FIRST, MOST2–4Remember → Evaluate
D5 — IAMDefinition, BEST, FIRST, MOST, Manager2–4Remember → Analyze
D6 — Assessment & TestingDefinition, BEST, FIRST, MOST, Manager2–4Remember → Evaluate
D7 — Security OperationsDefinition, FIRST, BEST, MOST, Manager2–4Remember → Analyze
D8 — Software Dev SecurityBEST, FIRST, Definition, MOST, Manager2–4Remember → Evaluate

Totals: 80 original items, 8 domains × 10, item-type mix is MCQ-4-option throughout (matches Sample exam template (legacy artifact) item_type_mix.mcq=0.85 — the remaining 15 % drag-drop / hotspot / scenario types are future authoring work).

Recommendations for the CISSP Master HTML question bank

  1. Backfill calculation questions in D1. The embedded question_bank.json is heavy on definition / scenario items; an additional 5–8 ALE / ROSI / EF / ARO arithmetic items would harden D1 quantitative coverage.
  2. Add MOST / FIRST framing variants. Many existing items use BEST framing; supplementing with MOST and FIRST variants exposes the candidate to the full wording-cue palette.
  3. Author the drag-drop / hotspot residual. The exam template allocates 5 % drag-drop and 3 % hotspot, but the engine currently only renders 4-option MCQs. Adding even a small set of structured-response items will exercise DEF-007 (the A–E keyboard cap) and force the engine to grow.
  4. Tag every item with pattern_type. The renderer already shows tags; adding a pattern_type facet enables targeted drills (e.g., "give me 20 FIRST-pattern items across all domains").
  5. Build a 25-item smoke-mock drawn equally from the eight domain-10 sets here. That gives the candidate a 90-minute, blueprint-conformant warm-up before the full 125-item simulation.

End of Track B initial pass — 80 original CISSP-style questions across all eight domains, plus per-domain pattern briefs, cross-domain summary, and authoring recommendations.

Track C Gaps 29,849 chars · 🧭 Track C Gaps tab

CISSP Track C — Alignment Check: Application vs Real Exam

Engagement: Functional + Psychometric audit of output/CISSP_Master.html Date: 2026-05-14 (IST) Baseline: ISC2 CISSP outline 2024-04-15 + AI Exam Guidance supplement 2026-04-02 (currency confirmed in working/isc2_currency_log.md, entry 2026-05-14 13:28 IST — no material change since baseline). Inputs to this analysis:

  • 🐞 Defect Log tab (20 defects + 5 coverage gaps)
  • 🎯 Track B Patterns tab (8 pattern briefs + 80 paraphrased items)
  • Functional Test Report (legacy, content merged here) (CAT engine behaviour summary)
  • Direct read of CAT engine internals in output/CISSP_Master.html (lines 10316–10961)
  • Sample exam template (legacy artifact) (readiness bands, item-type mix)

Risk legend (impact on the candidate's exam-day readiness):

  • High — app behaviour will materially mislead a candidate's preparation: reinforces wrong answer pattern, mis-allocates study time, or hides weakness.
  • Medium — app behaviour creates friction or distortion that an attentive candidate can self-correct, but a less-attentive one will not.
  • Low — cosmetic, latent, or quality-of-life misalignment; unlikely to change the pass/fail outcome.

1. Executive Summary

The application is directionally aligned with the post-2024-04-15 CISSP CAT: domain weights match (16/10/13/13/13/12/13/10), modes cover the three study activities a candidate actually needs (practice, weak-area drill, full-length CAT-100), and the embedded content correctly stratifies governance, lifecycle, architecture, network, IAM, assessment, operations, and SDLC themes. Track B confirms the question bank carries the right archetypes — risk arithmetic, governance-vs-management, due-care vs due-diligence, GDPR/HIPAA timeline drills, ethics-canon priority, BCDR RTO/RPO, IRT-aware difficulty curves.

Where the application diverges from the real exam, the divergences cluster in three areas:

  1. **CAT mechanics that are *similar enough to feel right but mathematically wrong*** — blueprint drift always dumped onto Domain 1 (DEF-001), session-scoped repeat protection conflated with 30-day exposure throttling (DEF-002), no persisted timer (DEF-003), silent session-truncation on pool exhaustion (DEF-013). These quietly distort what the candidate practises.
  2. **Answer-pattern emphasis that under-represents *manager / senior-management framing*** — the bank carries the patterns but the CAT engine does not preferentially serve them at higher theta, and the cold-start weak mode (DEF-014) cannot find them. A candidate who only drills the app risks over-learning technically correct answers and under-learning managerially correct answers, which is the single biggest reason strong technologists fail CISSP.
  3. **Rationale surfacing that conflates why this option wins with *what this option is***. The app's explanations describe the correct concept; the real exam rewards candidates who can articulate why the three distractors fail. Track B's per-item distractor notes are the right model; the app does not render them with equivalent prominence.

Net assessment: a candidate using only this application, starting from a strong technical baseline, faces a Medium risk of failing the real CAT — not because the content is wrong, but because the CAT experience over-rewards technical correctness and under-rewards executive judgement. With the recommendations in §5 implemented, that risk drops to Low. The currency log confirms the baseline is current; no content-side remediation is required from the 2024-04-15 / 2026-04-02 ISC2 updates beyond what is already merged.

2. Alignment Strengths (what the app gets right)

#StrengthEvidenceWhy it matters
S-01Domain weights match the 2024-04-15 JTA. D1=16, D2=10, D3=13, D4=13, D5=13, D6=12, D7=13, D8=10 (sums to 100).BLUEPRINT constant in master HTML; settings panel; working/build_master.py after the 2026-05-12 D8 11→10 fix.A candidate's practice distribution mirrors what the real CAT pulls from.
S-02Three study modes that map onto real candidate behaviour. Practice (unlimited drill), Weak (gap-targeted), CAT-100 (full simulation), Quick-20 (warm-up).`startSession('practice'\'weak'\'cat100'\'quick')` in master HTML.Mirrors the cycle most successful candidates use: triage → drill → simulate.
S-03IRT-aware difficulty drift. currentDifficulty starts at 3, +1 on correct / −1 on wrong, clamped 1–5; selection is `-\q.difficulty − target\+ jitter` over a top-5 pool.pickQuestion lines 10529–10554.Approximates the real CAT's theta tightening; teaches candidates to expect harder items as they get them right.
S-04Pattern coverage is faithful to Track B's archetype catalogue. Risk arithmetic, due-care/diligence, governance-vs-management, GDPR-72h / HIPAA-60d, ethics-canon priority, BCDR RTO/RPO, secure-SDLC stage ordering — all present.🎯 Track B Patterns tab Q-D1-01..Q-D8-10.The bank exercises the judgement layer the exam is built around.
S-05Manager-framing items exist for the governance domains (D1, D5, D7, D8).Track B Q-D1-02, Q-D1-06, Q-D1-08, Q-D1-10 (CFO/CISO framings); D5/D7/D8 manager items.Candidate is at least exposed to the senior-leader voice.
S-06Legal/regulatory timeline drills present (GDPR 72h, HIPAA 60d, breach notification by jurisdiction).Track B Q-D1-07, Q-D2 retention items, D7 incident-response timeline items.These are favourite trick details on the real exam.
S-07Heatmap surfaces weakness in the same shape as the exam blueprint (eight rows, green/yellow/red on 75/50 cutoffs).renderHeatmap in master HTML.A candidate can self-triage by domain without external tooling.
S-08SM-2 spaced repetition is implemented (intervals 1/6/round(prev*ef); ef floor 1.3; q=2 reset on wrong, q=4 on correct).sm2Update ~lines 10866–10881; storage in cissp_sm2_v2.Missed items don't get lost; aligns with cognitive-science consensus on retention.
S-09Currency banner mechanism is auditable. Idempotent block markers <!-- ISC2-YYYY-MM-DD start --> ... end --> in each domain recap; nightly log in working/isc2_currency_log.md.Master HTML domain recaps; currency log entries 2026-05-12 / 13 / 14.A future ISC2 outline refresh can be folded in without rewriting the file; the log proves the check ran.
S-102026-04-02 AI Exam Guidance is reflected. AI-mapped chips on D1/D2/D3/D5/D7/D8; D1 governance/risk panel covers EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 42001, NIST SP 800-218A, GDPR Art.22.Currency log entry 2026-05-12 20:11 IST; D1 reference panel.Candidates preparing for a Q3/Q4 2026 exam sitting see the supplement coverage they need.
S-11Readiness bands match a defensible reading of the 700/1000 pass standard. at_risk 0–599, watch 600–699, on_track 700–799, strong 800–1000.Sample exam template (legacy artifact).Avoids the common false-comfort trap of declaring "ready" at 65 % correct on practice.
S-12Single-file portable artefact. All 8 domain recaps, the CAT engine, the question bank, mnemonics, cheats, scenarios, decoder, and the 4-track engagement collateral merge into CISSP_Master.html (2.29 MB). No server, no network at runtime.File listing of output/; working/build_master.py.A candidate can study offline (commute, flights) — the most under-rated feature for a 100–150 h study plan.

3. Gap Catalogue (where the app and the real exam diverge)

Each gap is rated for candidate risk (will this materially affect a candidate's likelihood of passing?). Linkage to the defect log and Track B is shown.

3.1 CAT-mechanics gaps

IDGapRiskLinked defect / sourceWhy it matters on exam day
GAP-C01Blueprint drift dumped onto D1. For a 150-item simulated CAT, rounding drift of −2 is absorbed by D1 only; for a 125-item run, similar single-bucket overflow. Over many practice sessions, D1 is systematically over- or under-represented vs the published 16 %.MediumDEF-001Candidates who practise primarily at 125- or 150-item lengths under-train (or over-train) D1 governance items, which is the single most heavily-weighted domain.
GAP-C02Recent-30 exclusion leaks across sessions. pickQuestion reads STATE.history.slice(-30) as the exclusion set; this is cross-session history, not session-scoped. Two consecutive CAT runs share a ~30-item cooldown, silently shrinking the effective pool on the second run.MediumDEF-002The real ISC2 CAT does exposure-throttle, but on a ~30-day item-level window, not a 30-attempt sliding window. The app teaches a pool composition the exam will not reproduce.
GAP-C03No timer persistence across reload. timerEnd lives in module scope only; refresh during a CAT-100 either drops the timer or restarts it.HighDEF-003The real ISC2 CAT runs a 3-hour proctored clock. A candidate who practises with no realistic time pressure (because reloads reset it) will pace themselves wrongly on exam day.
GAP-C04Pool-exhaustion silent truncation. pickQuestion returns null if a domain bucket runs out at the required difficulty; loadNextQuestion treats null as "session done" and ends early. A CAT-100 can finish at 73 items with no explanation.HighDEF-013Practice scores computed on truncated sessions over-state readiness. A candidate sees "82 % on CAT-100" and walks in expecting to pass, having actually answered 73 items.
GAP-C05currentDifficulty resets every session. Per the Functional Test Report §3.2 gap #10. The real CAT carries theta forward within a single session but begins a new sitting from a calibrated start — the app, however, resets to 3 even mid-study-week.LowFR §3.2 #10A candidate's practice theta does not accumulate, so the engine cannot surface a "you're consistently strong at 4, try 5" growth signal.
GAP-C06Weak-mode cold start undefined. computeWeakDomain returns null when no domain has ≥3 attempts; startSession('weak') then runs as practice but is labelled "weak".MediumDEF-014A fresh candidate's first targeted drill is the moment of highest study leverage. The app silently fails this moment instead of routing the user to a quick 20-item baseline first.
GAP-C07Keyboard shortcuts cap at A–E. Voice and keyboard map A–E / 1–5; the schema permits 2–8 options (drag/drop, hotspot).LowDEF-007Real CAT is mouse-driven; keyboard-only friction is a practice-side gap, not an exam-day gap. Risk is to accessible-input candidates only.
GAP-C08Auto-submit on CAT-100 timer expiry is fragile. tickTimer calls alert() then endBtn?.click(); if the button isn't in the DOM, the session never finalises.LowDEF-019Rare DOM-mutation race; unlikely to bite a candidate at scale, but the real exam will always finalise on timer.

3.2 Answer-pattern emphasis gaps

IDGapRiskLinked sourceWhy it matters on exam day
GAP-P01No pattern_type tagging on bank items. Track B classifies each item as Calculation / BEST / FIRST / MOST / Manager / Definition / Legal / Ethics; the live bank in the master HTML carries no such field. The CAT engine cannot preferentially serve "Manager" or "MOST" items as a candidate's theta rises.HighTrack B header §"Style guarantee"; absence of pattern_type in CISSP_DATA.q_bank.A strong technologist whose theta climbs to 4–5 still gets randomly-typed items, mostly Definition/Calculation (the bank's modal types). The real exam tightens into judgement framing at high theta. The candidate practises the wrong skill at the wrong level.
GAP-P02D1 Calculation backfill needed (5–8 items). Track B flagged this explicitly. ROSI, three-variable ALE deltas, transfer-vs-acceptance break-even — under-represented in the live bank vs the 16 % weight.MediumTrack B "Next sweep priorities".D1 Calculation is one of the higher-yield study targets for borderline candidates; under-representation depresses preparation efficiency.
GAP-P03MOST / FIRST framing variants missing. Many items exist in BEST framing only. The exam systematically permutes BEST / FIRST / MOST / NEXT to differentiate candidates who memorised an answer from candidates who understood the concept.HighTrack B "Next sweep priorities".A candidate who learns "the answer to scenario X is option B" without distinguishing BEST (cost-balanced) from FIRST (sequence) from MOST (priority) loses ~5–10 marks on a real CAT.
GAP-P04No drag-drop / hotspot residual items. Sample exam template (legacy artifact) declares an item_type_mix including drag_drop and hotspot; the live bank is MCQ-only.LowTrack B "Next sweep priorities"; template JSON.Real CISSP CAT is overwhelmingly MCQ; residual item types appear infrequently. A surprise on exam day, but not a points sink.
GAP-P05Manager / senior-leader framing under-represented outside D1. D1 carries this archetype well. D4 (network) and D6 (assessment) skew technical; D3 (architecture) carries it for trust-models but not for design-trade-off framing.MediumTrack B per-domain briefs; reading of master HTML domain recap content.Candidates who pass because of technical depth and fail because of governance framing fail disproportionately in the technical-skewed domains, where their depth tempts them to pick the "most secure" rather than "most appropriate" answer.
GAP-P06No "BEST = balanced, MOST = priority" reading aid surfaced during practice. Track B pattern briefs articulate this; the app's rationale text usually does not.MediumTrack B Domain 1 brief §"Wording cues"; master HTML rationale rendering.The whole category of CISSP trick items hinges on this distinction; an explicit reading aid before/after each item would materially improve transfer.
GAP-P07Ethics-canon priority items light outside D1. Canon priority (society → honourable conduct → diligent service → profession) is examined in non-D1 contexts (e.g., D6 testing of a client system, D8 dual-loyalty in a vendor relationship).LowTrack B Q-D1-05 model; absence of canon framing in D6/D8 items.A handful of exam items hinge on this; under-representation costs 1–2 marks at the margin.

3.3 Rationale-surfacing gaps

IDGapRiskLinked sourceWhy it matters on exam day
GAP-R01Rationale = "why correct" only; distractor notes not surfaced equivalently. Track B's per-item structure — Rationale + Distractor notes for every wrong option — is the right model. The live bank in the master HTML typically renders one paragraph of correct-answer reasoning.HighCompare Track B Q-D1-01 distractor block to live bank rationale rendering in master HTML CAT pane.The exam rewards candidates who can articulate why three distractors are wrong, not only why one option is right. A candidate trained on correct-only rationale memorises answers; a candidate trained on distractor analysis transfers to novel scenarios.
GAP-R02No cross-reference to source book / page. question_bank_books.json carries a source field but the UI does not render it.LowFR §3.2 gap #8.A candidate who wants to read the underlying reference cannot navigate from a missed question to the relevant chapter.
GAP-R03No "wording cue" tag on items. Track B's per-domain wording-cue catalogue (BEST/FIRST/MOST/Manager/Calculation/Definition/Legal/Ethics) is not surfaced on item render.MediumTrack B domain briefs §"Wording cues" sections.A candidate practising at scale benefits from seeing the cue type alongside each item; over thousands of repetitions, the pattern becomes internalised. Without it, the pattern is implicit and slower to acquire.
GAP-R04No "managerial answer cue" highlight when an item is governance-framed. Items that hinge on "the manager would …" or "the BEST executive response …" carry no visual mark.MediumTrack B Q-D1-02, Q-D1-06, Q-D1-08, Q-D1-10.The single biggest reason strong technologists fail is selecting the technically correct option over the managerially correct option. A visual cue would slow them down at the right items.

3.4 Engine ↔ exam-behaviour conflicts

IDConflictRiskLinked defectWhy it matters
GAP-E01Item-selection scoring biases toward difficulty match, not domain blueprint. pickQuestion scoring `-\q.difficulty − target\+ jitter` selects on difficulty first, then random over a top-5 pool; the blueprint constraint is enforced via the plan (per-session quota) rather than as a per-pick weight. Combined with DEF-001 drift, the realised distribution drifts further.MediumDEF-001, DEF-013Practice distribution diverges from blueprint enough that a candidate's "I'm at 72 % overall" does not map to "I'd pass a real CAT".
GAP-E02No anchor-equating items. Real CAT carries un-scored anchor items for equating; the app cannot, and does not attempt to.Low(Not a defect — out of scope)Cosmetic. The candidate is not trained to expect "this one might be an anchor"; that's fine, because they shouldn't think about anchors mid-exam.
GAP-E03STATE.history truncated silently at 500. A candidate running a multi-month study program loses week-1 baseline.LowDEF-006Self-tracking gap, not exam-day gap.
GAP-E04CSV export misses theta_after, time_ms, mode, session_id. Per-item analytics gap.LowDEF-016A coach / study buddy cannot reconstruct theta trajectory from the export; doesn't change candidate's exam outcome.
GAP-E05No schema-versioning on cissp_cat_v2. A future migration risks wiping a candidate's mid-program state.LowDEF-020Latent risk; fires only on a future code update.

4. CAT-Behaviour-vs-Exam-Pattern Conflicts (synthesis)

Looking across the three gap groups above, the highest-risk combinations — where a CAT-mechanics gap reinforces an answer-pattern gap to create a candidate-side blind spot — are:

  1. DEF-001 (drift on D1) + GAP-P05 (manager-framing under-represented outside D1). Together: D1's manager items get over-served, then candidates generalise the manager pattern to D1 only and revert to technical answers in D4/D5/D7. Net candidate risk: Medium-High. Fix DEF-001 to rotate drift, and the pressure to over-balance manager items into D1 alone disappears.
  1. DEF-013 (silent truncation) + GAP-P03 (MOST/FIRST framing missing). A truncated CAT-100 might end before any MOST/FIRST items are served from the small subset available; the candidate scores 82 % on a 73-item run, all of which were BEST-framed Definition items. Net candidate risk: High. This is the single most dangerous combination in the current build.
  1. GAP-P01 (no pattern_type tagging) + GAP-R03 (no wording-cue tag) + GAP-R01 (rationale ≠ distractor analysis). Three independent surfacing gaps that compound into the core CISSP study failure mode: the candidate practises pattern recognition without ever seeing the pattern labels. Net candidate risk: High. Track B already supplies the tags and the distractor analyses; this is a rendering, not a content, problem.
  1. DEF-003 (timer non-persistence) + GAP-E01 (selection biased to difficulty over blueprint). A candidate who restarts a CAT-100 after a refresh ends up with two half-sessions whose composite distribution drifts further from blueprint than a single full session would. Net candidate risk: Medium.
  1. DEF-014 (weak-mode cold start) + GAP-P02 (D1 Calculation backfill needed). The candidate's first targeted drill is supposed to find their weakness; the cold-start path can't, so the candidate practises broadly when they should be practising D1 ROSI math (the highest-yield first-week study target). Net candidate risk: Medium.

5. Recommendations (concrete, in priority order)

Each recommendation is sized for a single dev iteration and linked to the defect(s) / gap(s) it closes.

5.1 P0 — close before next candidate cohort uses the app

#ActionClosesEffortExpected risk reduction
R-01Persist STATE.session.timerEnd and STATE.session.served on session start; rebind on init() if a session is mid-flight.DEF-003, GAP-C03SHigh → Low on timer realism
R-02Largest-remainder method for blueprint drift in buildBlueprintPlan: compute residuals weight − rounded/total for each domain; allocate the ±1 drift to the largest-residual domains.DEF-001, GAP-C01, GAP-E01SMedium → Low on D1 over-representation
R-03Relaxation ladder in pickQuestion when bucket exhausted: widen difficulty band → ignore recent-30 → substitute from nearest-weight domain → emit a session diagnostic. Never return null silently.DEF-013, GAP-C04MHigh → Low on truncated-session false comfort
R-04Render Track B distractor notes alongside the correct-answer rationale on every CAT item that has them (back-fill where missing).GAP-R01MHigh → Low on rationale-surfacing
R-05Tag every bank item with pattern_type (Calculation / BEST / FIRST / MOST / Manager / Definition / Legal / Ethics) and surface as a small chip below the stem.GAP-P01, GAP-R03, GAP-R04MHigh → Medium on pattern recognition

5.2 P1 — close before exam-week mock runs

#ActionClosesEffortExpected risk reduction
R-06Backfill 5–8 D1 Calculation items (ROSI, ALE-delta with three variables, transfer break-even).GAP-P02SMedium → Low on D1 Calculation
R-07Generate MOST / FIRST framing variants for the highest-traffic 20 items in the bank.GAP-P03, GAP-C03 (combination)MHigh → Medium on framing-pattern recognition
R-08Add ≥10 manager-framed items each to D4 / D6 / D7 (the technical-skewed domains).GAP-P05MMedium → Low on technical-skew failure mode
R-09Per-item exposure throttling. Replace slice(-30) with a cissp_exposure_v2 map of lastSeen[qid] = ts; exclude items where now − lastSeen < 30 days. Keep SESSION.served for in-session repeat protection.DEF-002, GAP-C02MMedium → Low on cross-session pool composition
R-10Weak-mode cold-start banner. If computeWeakDomain() === null, disable the Weak button and surface "Run Quick-20 first to identify your weak area."DEF-014, GAP-C06SMedium → Low on first-drill leverage

5.3 P2 — close before declaring the build "v1.0"

#ActionClosesEffort
R-11Replace alert() hint with inline role="status" aria-live="polite".DEF-004S
R-12Validate picked against q.options.length in submitAnswer.DEF-005S
R-13Extend keyboard shortcuts to A–H / 1–8 (dynamic from q.options.length).DEF-007S
R-14Surface "Showing X of Y" footer in Ctrl+K palette when truncated.DEF-008S
R-15visibilitychange throttling on refreshProgress interval.DEF-010S
R-16Debounce MutationObserver on #cat-stem with requestAnimationFrame; narrow to childList.DEF-012S
R-17Add popstate handler so Back/Forward re-invokes activate().DEF-017S
R-18Inline pre-DOMContentLoaded script in <head> to apply theme + font step before paint.DEF-015S
R-19Feature-detect SpeechRecognition once at init; hide voice button if absent.DEF-018S
R-20Extend exportCSV columns: session_id, mode, time_ms, theta_after.DEF-016S
R-21Add schemaVersion: 2 to cissp_cat_v2; migration switch in loadCatState.DEF-020S
R-22Raise STATE.history cap to 2000; surface a soft warning at ≥90 % full; promote exportCSV button.DEF-006S
R-23Guard sm2Update with __sm2_in_flight[qid] flag to neutralise rapid-double-click race.DEF-011S
R-24Replace tickTimer auto-submit endBtn?.click() with direct endSession() call; alert after state mutation.DEF-019S
R-25Add __palette_dirty invalidation flag on any future content mutation hook.DEF-009S

5.4 Coverage gaps requiring a browser (deferred)

#ActionReference
C-01One-shot axe-core run for live colour contrast.G-01
C-02NVDA + VoiceOver run for screen-reader announcement order.G-02
C-03Add @media print rules + watermark; visual print-preview.G-03
C-04Device measurement of .cat-opt touch targets at 360/390/414 viewports.G-04
C-05IndexedDB fallback design for localStorage quota approach.G-05

6. Linkage Matrix — Track A defects → Track C gaps → Candidate risk

DefectSeverity (A)Track C gapCandidate risk (C)
DEF-001MajorGAP-C01, GAP-E01Medium
DEF-002MajorGAP-C02Medium
DEF-003CriticalGAP-C03High
DEF-004Major(rationale UX)Low
DEF-005Major(input integrity)Low
DEF-006MinorGAP-E03Low
DEF-007MajorGAP-C07Low
DEF-008Minor(UX surfacing)Low
DEF-009Minor(latent)Low
DEF-010Minor(perf)Low
DEF-011Minor(SM-2 integrity)Low
DEF-012Minor(perf)Low
DEF-013MajorGAP-C04High
DEF-014MinorGAP-C06Medium
DEF-015Minor(cosmetic)Low
DEF-016MinorGAP-E04Low
DEF-017Minor(navigation)Low
DEF-018Minor(a11y)Low
DEF-019MinorGAP-C08Low
DEF-020MinorGAP-E05Low
Track B pattern observationTrack C gapCandidate risk
No pattern_type tagging on bank itemsGAP-P01High
D1 Calculation under-representedGAP-P02Medium
MOST / FIRST framing variants missingGAP-P03High
Drag-drop / hotspot residual missingGAP-P04Low
Manager framing under-represented in D4/D6/D7GAP-P05Medium
Wording-cue reading aid not surfacedGAP-P06, GAP-R03Medium
Ethics-canon priority light outside D1GAP-P07Low
Rationale = correct-only, no distractor analysisGAP-R01High
No source book/page cross-referenceGAP-R02Low
No managerial-cue highlight on governance itemsGAP-R04Medium

7. Candidate-side guidance (until the recommendations land)

Even with the current build, a disciplined candidate can compensate for the gaps above by:

  1. Treat every CAT-100 score as ±5 % uncertain. Cross-check by running two CAT-100s in different week-days; if both are ≥75 % you're more likely on-track than a single 80 % run. (Compensates for DEF-001, DEF-013, GAP-C04.)
  2. **After every missed question, write down why each of the other three options is wrong before reading the rationale.** This builds the distractor-analysis muscle the app does not exercise for you. (Compensates for GAP-R01.)
  3. **For every item, identify the wording cue explicitly** (BEST / FIRST / MOST / Manager / Calculation / Definition / Legal / Ethics) before answering. Pin the eight cues on your study desk. (Compensates for GAP-P01, GAP-R03.)
  4. In any technically-framed scenario (D3/D4/D6), pause and ask "what would a CISO answer here?" before picking. Default to risk-proportionate over maximalist. (Compensates for GAP-P05.)
  5. Track your own theta on paper. Note your subjective difficulty experience per session; if every item felt easy, the practise difficulty drifted low — bias toward starting your next session in quick mode at higher difficulty to compensate. (Compensates for GAP-C05.)
  6. **Run at least one CAT-100 *without ever refreshing the page*** to experience real time pressure. Sit it like the real exam. (Compensates for DEF-003.)
  7. Read Track B's pattern briefs once a week — they're the missing UI overlay for the bank you're already drilling. (Compensates for GAP-P06.)

8. Overall verdict

DimensionBefore recommendationsAfter P0 + P1
Domain-weight realismAligned (drift acknowledged)Aligned
Mode coverageAlignedAligned
Pattern coverage (Track B archetypes)Aligned in content, mis-surfaced in UXAligned
Manager-framing emphasisUnder-represented outside D1Balanced
Distractor-analysis surfacingWeakStrong
Timer realismBroken on reloadPersistent
Pool integrity under exhaustionSilent failureRelaxation ladder + diagnostic
Exposure throttlingCross-session leakPer-item 30-day window
Currency to 2024-04-15 + 2026-04-02Current (audit log)Current
Candidate risk of failing real CATMediumLow

The Track A defect log, Track B pattern bank, and this Track C alignment analysis are mutually consistent: every recommendation above traces back to a specific defect ID or a specific Track B pattern observation, and every elevated candidate risk is grounded in a defect/pattern pair rather than a hypothetical. The path from Medium → Low candidate risk is five P0 actions + five P1 actions — fifteen developer-days of work at the outside.

End of Track C — 🧭 Track C Gaps tab. Cross-references: 🐞 Defect Log tab (Track A), 🎯 Track B Patterns tab (Track B), Functional Test Report (legacy, content merged here), Sample exam template (legacy artifact), working/isc2_currency_log.md.

Smoke Mock 25 16,258 chars · 🔥 Smoke Mock 25 tab

CISSP 25-Item Smoke-Mock — Blueprint-Weighted Warm-Up

Source: 🎯 Track B Patterns tab (80-item Track B set). Duration target: 90 minutes (≈ 3.6 min / item). Style: mirrors the real CISSP per-item pace and the published CBK weight mix. Audience: CISSP candidate, ~2 weeks before exam day. Use after the 8 domain-10 drills. Author: QA agent (autonomous), 2026-05-13.

Blueprint distribution (rounded to 25 items)

DomainCBK weightItems in this mock
D1 — Security & Risk Management16 %4
D2 — Asset Security10 %3
D3 — Security Architecture & Engineering13 %3
D4 — Communication & Network Security13 %3
D5 — Identity & Access Management13 %3
D6 — Security Assessment & Testing12 %3
D7 — Security Operations13 %3
D8 — Software Development Security10 %3
Total100 %25

Pattern-type mix in this mock: Calculation × 2, BEST × 9, FIRST × 2, MOST × 8, Definition × 4, Manager × 5, Legal × 2, Ethics × 1 (some items carry two tags). Bloom span: Remember → Evaluate. Difficulty span: 2 → 4 (target_mean ≈ 3.1).

How to take this mock

  1. Time-box 90 minutes. Do NOT pause. Treat as exam conditions.
  2. Answer all 25; do not skip. If unsure, mark "BEST guess" mentally and move on.
  3. After the timer ends, check answers against §"Answer key" at the bottom.
  4. Score yourself: ≥ 80 % (20+) → on-track readiness. 70–79 % → watch. < 70 % → at-risk; rerun the weak-domain drill in 🎯 Track B Patterns tab.
  5. For every miss, re-read the rationale and the distractor notes in the source file. The distractor notes are where most of the studying happens.

Questions

Q-01 (D1, difficulty 2, Apply, Calculation)

A retail company values its customer-data store at $1,500,000. A ransomware threat is assessed with an exposure factor of 40 % and an annualised rate of occurrence of 0.25. What is the annualised loss expectancy?

  • A. $150,000
  • B. $375,000
  • C. $600,000
  • D. $1,500,000

Q-02 (D1, difficulty 4, Evaluate, MOST / Ethics)

A CISSP-certified consultant discovers, while testing a client system, that the client is intentionally falsifying revenue figures in a way that materially misleads investors. Per the ISC2 Code of Ethics, what is the MOST appropriate action?

  • A. Continue the engagement; the financial fraud is outside the scope of the security test.
  • B. Report the matter to law enforcement immediately, bypassing the client entirely.
  • C. Raise the matter through the client's audit committee and, if not addressed, fulfil the canon to protect society — including reporting through appropriate channels.
  • D. Resign from the engagement quietly and take no further action.

Q-03 (D1, difficulty 3, Apply, Legal)

Your organisation suffers a confirmed personal-data breach involving EU data subjects. Under GDPR, you must notify the supervisory authority within how many hours of becoming aware?

  • A. 24
  • B. 48
  • C. 72
  • D. 96

Q-04 (D1, difficulty 4, Analyze, BEST / Calculation)

A company's quantitative risk assessment shows that a particular threat has an ALE of $40,000. A proposed control costs $60,000 per year and reduces the ALE to $5,000. The CFO asks the CISO whether to implement it. The CISO's BEST response is:

  • A. "Implement the control — security is worth the investment regardless of cost."
  • B. "Do not implement the control as-is — annual cost exceeds annual loss reduction. We should evaluate cheaper alternatives, risk acceptance, or transfer."
  • C. "Defer the decision; we need a five-year discounted cash-flow analysis before deciding."
  • D. "Accept the risk silently — quantitative risk methods are unreliable."

Q-05 (D2, difficulty 2, Understand, Definition)

Two non-sensitive datasets are merged into a single analytics warehouse. After the merge, the combined dataset would allow an attacker to identify individuals. The MOST appropriate response is:

  • A. Continue treating the dataset as non-sensitive because each source was non-sensitive.
  • B. Re-classify the merged dataset at the higher protection level dictated by the aggregation risk.
  • C. Delete the warehouse — aggregation is forbidden.
  • D. Encrypt one of the source datasets at rest before merging.

Q-06 (D2, difficulty 3, Apply, Legal)

A European subsidiary of a US-headquartered company plans to store EU customer data in a US-based data centre. What is the MOST important consideration?

  • A. Local data-protection law applies based on where the data subjects reside and where the data is processed — adequate transfer mechanisms (e.g., SCCs, adequacy decisions) are required.
  • B. Only US law applies because the parent company is US-headquartered.
  • C. Only EU law applies because the data subjects are European.
  • D. The lower of the two regulatory burdens governs.

Q-07 (D2, difficulty 4, Analyze, MOST / Manager)

A research team requests access to production customer records to test a new ML model. What is the MOST appropriate response?

  • A. Grant access — the team needs realistic data.
  • B. Refuse access — production data must never be used.
  • C. Provide anonymised or synthetic data; if real data is unavoidable, use masking / tokenisation with documented business justification, owner approval, and time-bounded access.
  • D. Provide production data but require the team to sign an NDA.

Q-08 (D3, difficulty 2, Remember, Definition)

The Bell-LaPadula Star (*) property states:

  • A. No read up.
  • B. No write down.
  • C. No read down.
  • D. No write up.

Q-09 (D3, difficulty 4, Analyze, MOST)

A data-centre is being designed to prioritise life safety of personnel during a fire. Door access controls should be configured as:

  • A. Fail-safe (doors unlock or open on power failure / alarm).
  • B. Fail-secure (doors lock on power failure).
  • C. Multi-factor on egress.
  • D. Magnetically locked in both states.

Q-10 (D3, difficulty 4, Analyze, BEST)

A company is choosing a cryptographic primitive for secure firmware update verification on a constrained IoT device. The BEST choice is:

  • A. Symmetric AES-CBC with a device-stored key.
  • B. Digital signature using ECDSA (or Ed25519) — the device holds the manufacturer's public key.
  • C. SHA-256 hash of the firmware embedded in the image.
  • D. HMAC-SHA256 with a shared secret stored on every device.

Q-11 (D4, difficulty 3, Apply, BEST)

The BEST wireless authentication mode for a corporate office of 5,000 employees with managed devices is:

  • A. WPA2-PSK with a strong shared passphrase rotated annually.
  • B. WPA2 / WPA3 Enterprise with 802.1X and EAP-TLS using device certificates.
  • C. Open SSID with a captive-portal sign-in.
  • D. MAC-address filtering only.

Q-12 (D4, difficulty 4, Analyze, MOST)

Two business units have hosts in the same VLAN. A compliance auditor notes that the units handle data of different classifications. The MOST appropriate response is:

  • A. Add a sign on the rack warning users to be careful.
  • B. Implement L3 segmentation with a firewall or microsegmentation between the units; VLANs alone are administrative, not a security boundary.
  • C. Encrypt all data at rest on every host.
  • D. Move both units to the public cloud.

Q-13 (D4, difficulty 4, Analyze, BEST)

A network team wants to move toward Zero Trust. Which principle is MOST aligned with that goal?

  • A. Build a stronger perimeter firewall.
  • B. Never trust, always verify — authenticate and authorise every request based on identity, device, and context, regardless of network location.
  • C. Place all internal services on the same flat network.
  • D. Disable multi-factor authentication for internal users to reduce friction.

Q-14 (D5, difficulty 3, Apply, BEST)

A web app must let users sign in with their personal Google account and then call a third-party calendar API on the user's behalf. The BEST protocol mix is:

  • A. SAML 2.0 only.
  • B. OIDC for authentication + OAuth 2.0 for authorisation to the calendar API.
  • C. Kerberos.
  • D. Direct LDAP bind to Google.

Q-15 (D5, difficulty 4, Analyze, MOST / Manager)

An employee transfers from finance to engineering. Three months later, an audit shows the user still has finance application access. Which JML failure is MOST likely?

  • A. Joiner.
  • B. Mover (entitlements were added at the new role but not removed for the old role).
  • C. Leaver.
  • D. None — three months of overlap is normal.

Q-16 (D5, difficulty 4, Analyze, MOST / Manager)

A long-running batch service runs as a shared service account with a static password embedded in scripts. The MOST significant control gap is:

  • A. The password is short.
  • B. Loss of accountability and inability to rotate without coordinated downtime — recommend a managed-identity / vaulted credential with auditable retrieval.
  • C. The service runs at night.
  • D. The script is in version control.

Q-17 (D6, difficulty 2, Remember, Definition)

A SOC 2 Type II report covers:

  • A. The financial controls of the audited organisation.
  • B. The design of the audited organisation's controls at a single point in time.
  • C. The design and operating effectiveness of the audited organisation's controls over the trust services criteria across a defined period (typically 6–12 months).
  • D. A public-relations summary of the organisation's security posture.

Q-18 (D6, difficulty 4, Analyze, FIRST / Manager)

A pen-test report lists 50 findings. The FIRST step the security team should take is:

  • A. Email every owner asking them to fix everything by Friday.
  • B. Triage by exploitability, blast radius, and business impact, then prioritise remediation with owners.
  • C. Re-test all findings immediately.
  • D. Publish the report on the company intranet.

Q-19 (D6, difficulty 4, Evaluate, BEST / Manager)

A vulnerability scanner reports 2,300 findings. Roughly 40 % are duplicates and 30 % are false positives. The BEST next action is:

  • A. Patch every reported finding regardless.
  • B. De-duplicate, validate, prioritise, and tune the scanner; track time-to-remediate against severity-based SLAs.
  • C. Switch to a different scanner immediately.
  • D. Disable the scanner — too noisy.

Q-20 (D7, difficulty 2, Remember, Definition)

The correct order of the incident-response lifecycle is:

  • A. Detect → Eradicate → Contain → Recover.
  • B. Prepare → Detect → Contain → Eradicate → Recover → Lessons Learned.
  • C. Contain → Prepare → Detect → Eradicate → Recover.
  • D. Detect → Recover → Contain → Eradicate.

Q-21 (D7, difficulty 3, Apply, FIRST)

You confirm an active ransomware encryption process on a file server. What should be done FIRST?

  • A. Open a ticket with the antivirus vendor.
  • B. Isolate the host from the network (disable network interface, pull the cable, or quarantine via EDR).
  • C. Image the disk for forensics.
  • D. Restore from backup over the encryption.

Q-22 (D7, difficulty 4, Evaluate, BEST)

Backups are stored on the same SAN as production. The BEST mitigation against ransomware is:

  • A. Increase backup frequency.
  • B. Add an offline / immutable copy (air-gapped tape, immutable object lock) and test restore regularly.
  • C. Encrypt the backups in place.
  • D. Move backups to a different folder on the SAN.

Q-23 (D8, difficulty 2, Apply, BEST)

A new search feature concatenates user input into a SQL query. The BEST control is:

  • A. Validate the input length.
  • B. Parameterised queries / prepared statements with bound variables; combine with least-privilege DB account.
  • C. Add a WAF in front of the application.
  • D. Hash the user input before insertion.

Q-24 (D8, difficulty 4, Analyze, MOST)

An API endpoint returns a customer record when a numeric customerId is supplied. Any authenticated user can request any customerId and receive the data. The MOST accurate vulnerability description is:

  • A. SQL injection.
  • B. Broken Object-Level Authorisation (BOLA / IDOR) — the server fails to verify the requester's authorisation for the requested object.
  • C. Cross-site scripting.
  • D. XML external entity.

Q-25 (D8, difficulty 4, Evaluate, MOST / Manager)

A development team is asked to "add security to the pipeline". They propose running a manual pen test once a quarter and nothing else. The MOST appropriate executive response is:

  • A. Approve — quarterly pen testing is sufficient.
  • B. Reject — propose a defence-in-depth shift-left: SAST / SCA / secrets scanning on commit, container & IaC scans on build, DAST and integration tests on deploy, plus periodic pen tests and threat modelling at design; pen tests alone are sparse, late, and expensive.
  • C. Reject — only adopt DAST.
  • D. Approve and reduce frequency to annual.

Answer key (with one-line rationales)

#Q-ID (Track B)DomainCorrectOne-line rationale
1Q-D1-01D1AALE = (AV × EF) × ARO = 1,500,000 × 0.40 × 0.25 = 150,000.
2Q-D1-05D1CCanon 1 (society) overrides canon 3 (principal); escalate via governance first.
3Q-D1-07D1CGDPR Article 33 — 72 hours after awareness.
4Q-D1-08D1BSaved ALE = 35k < annual cost 60k → net −25k; seek cheaper option, accept, or transfer.
5Q-D2-01D2BAggregation changes sensitivity profile; re-classify upward.
6Q-D2-04D2AData sovereignty — both jurisdictions apply; SCCs / adequacy required for transfer.
7Q-D2-10D2CLeast privilege + data minimisation; anonymise / synthesise first.
8Q-D3-01D3BBLP star = "no write down" (preserves confidentiality).
9Q-D3-04D3ALife safety overrides confidentiality — fail-safe doors unlock on alarm.
10Q-D3-10D3BAsymmetric verification means device never holds a secret; manufacturer's private key signs.
11Q-D4-02D4B802.1X / EAP-TLS gives per-device certificates and per-user accountability.
12Q-D4-05D4BVLAN is administrative; security boundary needs inspection at L3.
13Q-D4-09D4BZero Trust = continuous per-request verification, not perimeter trust.
14Q-D5-02D5BOIDC = who (ID token); OAuth = what (access token); modern consumer-federation pattern.
15Q-D5-06D5BMover failure → privilege creep; periodic entitlement reviews catch it.
16Q-D5-10D5BShared static credentials break IAAA's accountability leg; use workload identity / vaulted secret.
17Q-D6-01D6CType II = design + operating effectiveness across a period (6–12 months).
18Q-D6-04D6BTriage by exploitability + blast radius + impact; map findings to owners.
19Q-D6-10D6BTune the signal first; false-positive reduction is itself a metric.
20Q-D7-01D7BPrepare → Detect → Contain → Eradicate → Recover → Lessons Learned.
21Q-D7-02D7BContain first — stop the bleeding before forensics or restore.
22Q-D7-08D7BSame-SAN backups get co-encrypted; offline / immutable breaks blast radius.
23Q-D8-01D8BParameterisation separates code from data at the SQL boundary.
24Q-D8-04D8BBOLA / IDOR — authn passed but per-object authz missing.
25Q-D8-10D8BDefence-in-depth + shift-left catches issues at cheapest moment; pen test alone is sparse and late.

Scoring band (per Sample exam template (legacy artifact) readiness model)

Raw score (out of 25)Approx. scaled (out of 1000)Band
0–14< 600At-risk
15–17600–699Watch
18–20700–799On-track
21–25800–1000Strong

A passing scaled score on the real CISSP is 700, so 18 / 25 (≈ 72 %) on this mock is the minimum "you're close" signal. ≥ 21 / 25 (≈ 84 %) is the comfort-zone target for sitting the exam within two weeks.

End of 25-item smoke-mock. Source rationales and distractor notes live in 🎯 Track B Patterns tab.
